wines.name: Tío Pepe Fino
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts in a solera-criadera system of 5 scales; average age 4-5 years under flor

taste.finish: clean and briny with persistent flor character
taste.summary: Pale straw Fino aged under a thick flor yeast blanket in Jerez. Bone dry with saline freshness, green apple, toasted almond and a bright citrus lift.

pairings.why: The wine's saline almond notes mirror the sweet nuttiness of cured Iberian ham.
High acidity cuts through the oil; the flor yeast enhances the sea-mineral match.
The bright acidity and herbal notes track the cold tomato-and-pepper soup.
Saline minerality and low ABV make this a versatile raw-fish partner.

history.summary: Named after Manuel María González Ángel's uncle José Ángel González (Tío Pepe), who introduced the concept of a dry Fino to the market. Aged through a solera-criadera system established in the 1840s, with flor yeast maintained year-round in González Byass's historic Cathedral bodega.

milestones.event: First Tío Pepe Fino bottling documented under the house of González Byass
Famous illuminated Tío Pepe sign erected on Madrid's Puerta del Sol
González Byass reacquired full ownership of the company, securing the brand's continuity

wines.description: Flagship Fino from González Byass, aged under flor yeast in Jerez since the 1840s. Dry, saline and pale with almond and citrus notes.

wines.name: Tío Pepe En Rama
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; minimal clarification and filtration before bottling in spring

taste.finish: long and waxy with persistent flor complexity
taste.summary: Seasonally released unfiltered version of Tío Pepe drawn from the most active flor scales. Greater texture and flor complexity than the standard bottling.

pairings.why: The waxy flor texture and ocean brine echo the sea anemone's marine richness.
The crisp shrimp-and-chickpea pancake is lifted by the wine's saline freshness.
Classic aperitivo pairing that amplifies the wine's own almond-and-brine character.

history.summary: The En Rama release was pioneered by González Byass as a seasonal bottling of Tío Pepe drawn in early spring when the flor veil is at peak thickness, before summer heat thins it. Minimal fining and no cold stabilisation preserve the wine's natural haze and maximum flor complexity.

milestones.event: First annual En Rama seasonal bottling introduced by González Byass

The Del Castillo Amontillado comes from the cellar of a named almacenista family.

wines.name: East India Solera Cream
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; blend of old Oloroso and Pedro Ximénez; aged and then submitted to a sea voyage ageing simulation in elevated temperatures

taste.summary: Lustau's East India Solera evokes the historical 'Manila Galleon' practice of shipping sherry in barrels across the tropics to India, which accelerated ageing. Rich, sweet and complex with raisins, toffee and vanilla.

pairings.why: The wine's raisin and vanilla warmth amplifies the similar notes in the dessert.

history.summary: The East India Solera revives the 17th and 18th century practice of sending sherry in barrels on long sea voyages to the Indies, where the rocking motion, humidity changes and tropical heat accelerated ageing. Lustau replicates this by subjecting old Oloroso blended with PX to gently elevated temperatures, deepening colour and sweetness.

wines.name: Inocente Fino

history.summary: Inocente is fermented and aged from a single named vineyard (Macharnudo Alto) without blending with other pagos, a rare practice in Sherry production. The solera was established in the late 19th century and Inocente is also distinguished by primary fermentation in wooden oak vessels rather than stainless steel.

wines.name: El Candado Pedro Ximénez

pairings.why: The concentrated sweetness and dark-fruit warmth of PX on cold vanilla is the quintessential Jerez dessert combination.
The viscous sweetness matches the richness of deep-fried dough and chocolate dipping sauce.
The intense sweetness cuts through the salt and pungency of Asturian blue cheese.

history.summary: El Candado ('the padlock') PX takes its name from the padlocked casks that Valdespino used to mark their most precious Pedro Ximénez barrels. The solera is maintained from sun-dried PX grapes from the Montilla-Moriles region, pressed and fermented at Valdespino before ageing through the oxidative solera in Jerez.

wines.description: Valdespino El Candado PX from a solera founded in 1910. Intensely sweet with concentrated raisins, dark chocolate and warm spice.

verified.address_quoted: Jerez de la Frontera, Cádiz, Spain

wines.name: Amontillado VORS
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; VORS certified minimum 30 years average age from old soleras acquired by Bodegas Tradición at founding; dual biological then oxidative ageing

pairings.why: The wine's complex oxidative depth and full body hold up to intensely flavoured game bird.
Hard Spanish sheep's milk cheese with the complexity to match this very old wine.
Nut aromatics in the wine resonate with roasted autumn nuts.

history.summary: Bodegas Tradición was founded in 1998 with the explicit mission of releasing only VORS and VOS aged sherries from old soleras purchased from retiring families or dormant bodegas in the Jerez triangle. The Amontillado VORS is sourced from soleras originally established decades earlier; Tradición ages, blends and bottles without compromise to commercial volume.

VORS Amontillado certified under the new Consejo Regulador classification

wines.name: Oloroso VORS
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; VORS certified minimum 30 years average age; fully oxidative ageing

pairings.why: The wine's dark, complex depth and full body match the intensity of blood sausage.
Powerful, creamy Spanish sheep's milk cheese is well matched by very old Oloroso.
The coffee-and-bitter-chocolate register of old Oloroso mirrors fine dark chocolate confections.

history.summary: Like all wines in the Bodegas Tradición range, the Oloroso VORS is built from soleras of documented great age acquired when Tradición was founded in 1998. The house produces no young or standard commercial sherry; every wine in the portfolio is VORS or VOS certified. Production volumes are deliberately small to protect solera integrity.

VORS Oloroso certified by the Consejo Regulador

wines.name: Palo Cortado VORS
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; VORS certified minimum 30 years average age; arose from Fino barrels whose flor died naturally before continuing oxidative ageing

pairings.why: The wine's full body and bitter-orange complexity pair with marinated game bird.
The wine's orange-bitters note interacts beautifully with quince membrillo alongside mature sheep's cheese.
The wine's full, dry structure supports the rich fat of top-quality pork.

history.summary: Palo Cortado is among the most elusive of sherry styles, arising spontaneously when Fino barrels unexpectedly lose their flor. Tradición acquires old Palo Cortado soleras and maintains them at VORS level. The wine's dual-nature heritage ,  biological start, oxidative finish ,  produces an aromatics-palate contrast that no other wine style achieves.

milestones.event: Old Palo Cortado soleras acquired and bottled by Bodegas Tradición
VORS Palo Cortado certified by the Consejo Regulador

wines.name: Pedro Ximénez VOS

Aged sheep's milk with salt and crystalline texture is cut by the extreme sweetness of old PX.
Coffee and dark fruit notes amplify the bittersweet molten chocolate centre.

history.summary: Bodegas Tradición's Pedro Ximénez VOS is one of the rarest aged PX wines in Jerez, coming from a solera of documented 20-plus years average age. The VOS (Vinum Optimum Signatum) certification requires isotopic analysis by the Consejo Regulador. As with all Tradición wines, no effort is made to increase volume at the expense of age or quality.

wines.name: La Bota de Fino
wines.oak_regime: American oak bota (butt); single-cask selection from an established Jerez or Sanlúcar bodega; unfiltered; numbered saca edition

pairings.why: Saline minerality and high acidity from the flor perfectly complement raw oysters.
The wine's saline depth matches the oceanic character of steamed clams.
The flor complexity and almond notes of the wine enhance the nuttiness of top-quality cured ham.

history.summary: Equipo Navazos was founded in 2005 by Jesús Barquín and Eduardo Ojeda as a consortium of enthusiasts buying and bottling single barrels from exceptional soleras across the Jerez and Sanlúcar bodegas. Each La Bota bottling is numbered (La Bota #1, #2, etc.) and released in limited quantities directly from the cask without fining or filtration. Each number represents a unique single-barrel saca from a different solera.

milestones.event: Equipo Navazos founded by Jesús Barquín and Eduardo Ojeda; first La Bota releases
Over 50 numbered La Bota editions released
Over 135 numbered La Bota editions released across all sherry styles

wines.description: Equipo Navazos single-cask unfiltered Fino, numbered La Bota edition. Greater natural complexity than commercial Finos, saline and dry with almond and flor character.

wines.name: La Bota de Manzanilla
wines.oak_regime: American oak bota; single-cask from a Sanlúcar bodega; unfiltered; numbered saca edition

pairings.why: The ocean-adjacent pairings of Sanlúcar Manzanilla and the prized local prawns is a Jerez gastronomic classic.
Atlantic barnacles and an Atlantic-born wine: saline intensity amplified.
The intensity of salt-cured tuna finds a saline peer in single-cask Manzanilla.

history.summary: Each La Bota de Manzanilla edition is selected from a single bota in Sanlúcar de Barrameda, where Atlantic proximity intensifies the flor veil and creates the most saline and delicate of all biological sherries. Equipo Navazos bottles without dilution or filtration to capture the maximum character of the individual barrel.

wines.description: Equipo Navazos single-cask unfiltered Manzanilla from Sanlúcar, numbered La Bota edition. Intensely saline and delicate with maximum flor character.

wines.name: La Bota de Amontillado

pairings.why: Old Spanish tradition: a cup of Amontillado alongside a chickpea and meat broth.
The wine's bitter almond and dry tang complements the challenging artichoke pairing.
Earthy, savoury depth in the wine mirrors the umami intensity of mushroom and truffle.

history.summary: La Bota de Amontillado editions are selected from barrels where the flor has died and the wine has begun its oxidative ageing phase in a specific Jerez bodega. Equipo Navazos identifies the exceptional individual botas through repeated tasting before each numbered saca and bottles without fining or filtration, making each edition a unique snapshot.

milestones.event: First La Bota de Amontillado edition released by Equipo Navazos

wines.description: Equipo Navazos single-cask unfiltered Amontillado, numbered La Bota edition. Roasted hazelnuts, beeswax and a long complex dry finish.

wines.name: La Bota de Palo Cortado
wines.oak_regime: American oak bota; single-cask Palo Cortado selected from a Jerez bodega; unfiltered; numbered saca edition

pairings.why: The wine's full body and orange-bitter register are natural counterpoints to the richness of confit duck.
Full body and dry structure support the marbled richness of premium Iberian pork cuts.
Nutty aromatics and dried fruit in the wine find their mirror in the traditional Andalusian combination.

history.summary: Palo Cortado is the rarest sherry style, arising spontaneously when a Fino barrel's flor dies unexpectedly before the wine is fortified to Oloroso levels. Equipo Navazos specifically searches for these exceptional barrels in established bodegas, buying single botas to release unfiltered as numbered La Bota de Palo Cortado editions.

milestones.event: First La Bota de Palo Cortado released by Equipo Navazos

Classic Andalusian dessert pairing that amplifies the wine's own nut and dried fruit character.

history.summary: La Bota de Oloroso editions are selected from fully oxidative botas that have never been in contact with flor. Equipo Navazos tastes through warehouses looking for individual barrels of exceptional depth and complexity, bottling without dilution or filtration as numbered single-cask editions.

history.summary: La Bota de Pedro Ximénez editions are single-cask bottlings from old PX soleras in Jerez bodegas, selected by Equipo Navazos for exceptional concentration and complexity. The numbered editions document the unique character of each individual bota ,  something impossible in commercial blended production.

The Amontillado VORS solera at Sánchez Romate was established in the early 20th century and is one of the oldest active Amontillado soleras in Jerez. VORS certification confirms minimum 30 years average age through isotopic analysis.

wines.name: Dry Sack Medium

taste.summary: A classic medium sherry, the style that made Williams & Humbert famous. Off-dry with dried apricot, almond toffee and gentle spice from a blend of Amontillado, Oloroso and PX soleras.

history.summary: Dry Sack was created in 1906 by Williams & Humbert to satisfy demand for a medium-style sherry in the British market. The name is a deliberate play on dry sherry (suggesting dryness while actually being medium sweet) and the traditional Spanish term 'saca' (drawn off from the solera). It became one of the widely distributed sherries in the UK through the mid-20th century.

Dry Sack establishes Williams & Humbert as a major UK sherry brand

history.summary: Don Zoilo is the premium range of Williams & Humbert, established to offer a step up from the commercial Dry Sack medium. The Fino was introduced in the 1950s as a classic Jerez biological sherry intended for the Spanish domestic market and specialist exports, where dry Fino style was preferred over the sweet medium style popular in Britain.

The name Jalifa refers to a traditional Moroccan title used for the viceroy of the Spanish protectorate of Morocco, reflecting Jerez's historical trade connections with North Africa.

history.summary: Dos Cortados ('two cut ones') takes its name from the tradition of marking Palo Cortado barrels with two diagonal lines (palos cortados) on the head to indicate their special character. history.summary: La Panesa is named after the pago (vineyard plot) in Sanlúcar's Balbaina zone where Vinicola Hidalgo sources its Palomino Fino grapes from calcareous albariza soils. history.summary: Gobernador ('governor') Oloroso is named after the historic administrative officers of the region during Jerez's golden export era. Vinicola Hidalgo established the Oloroso solera in the late 19th century as a fully oxidative wine from Sanlúcar's Atlantic-influenced cellars.

history.summary: El Maestro Sierra (meaning 'the sierra master') was founded around 1830 in a bodega in the Jerez historic centre and has been associated with the Borrego family for generations. The estate produces extremely limited quantities from very old soleras in traditional conditions, with no industrial infrastructure.

history.summary: The Amontillado 12 Años from El Maestro Sierra is produced from old soleras in the historic centre bodega. wines.name: Antique Palo Cortado
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; single-solera Palo Cortado; Antique range; arose from Fino barrels with failed flor; average 15 years

pairings.why: The wine's full body and orange-bitters register are a natural match for roasted small birds.
The wine's hazelnut and orange notes are mirrored in this classic Andalusian snack combination.
The bitter-orange and walnut depth of Palo Cortado works well with aged Italian sheep's cheese and honey.

history.summary: Antique Palo Cortado from Fernando de Castilla is sourced from barrels in the Santiago bodega where the flor died spontaneously during biological ageing. These exceptional barrels are maintained as a separate Palo Cortado solera without blending, presented at natural alcohol and colour with minimal manipulation.

milestones.event: Antique Palo Cortado introduced in the Fernando de Castilla Antique range

wines.description: Fernando de Castilla Antique Palo Cortado, unblended from a single Jerez solera. Orange marmalade and hazelnuts nose, full-bodied walnut oil and long bittersweet finish.

verified.address_quoted: C/Jardinillo, 7 al 11, Jerez de la Frontera-11404, Cádiz. SPAIN

wines.name: Antique Pedro Ximénez
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; single-solera PX; Antique range; oxidative ageing; average 10-12 years

pairings.why: The wine's treacle and dark-fruit intensity amplify warm chocolate pudding.
Extreme sweetness and viscosity cut through the strong salt and fat of Asturian blue cheese.
Dried-fig notes in the PX mirror the filling of this classic Andalusian-style pastry.

history.summary: Antique Pedro Ximénez completes the Antique range with a single-solera PX from the Santiago bodega. Like all Antique wines, it is bottled without the cold stabilisation or heavy filtration typical of commercial sherry, preserving the natural concentration and depth of old sun-dried Pedro Ximénez.

milestones.event: Antique Pedro Ximénez introduced in the Fernando de Castilla Antique range

wines.description: Fernando de Castilla Antique PX, unblended from a single Jerez solera. Near-black, intensely sweet with concentrated raisins, dark chocolate and a long viscous finish.

verified.address_quoted: C/Jardinillo, 7 al 11, Jerez de la Frontera-11404, Cádiz. SPAIN

wines.name: Royal Esmeralda Fino
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; biological ageing under flor; Sandeman Jerez fine Fino solera; average 4-5 years

pairings.why: Classic Fino aperitivo pairing: almond notes meet the saltiness of ham and olives.
Saline freshness and high acidity cut through garlic oil and complement the ocean character of prawns.
Fresh, mild sheep's milk cheese is matched by the light, dry character of the Fino.

history.summary: Royal Esmeralda Fino is Sandeman Jerez's premium dry Fino, positioned above the house's standard Character range as a more refined expression of biological ageing. The Sandeman brand was founded in 1790 by George Sandeman and is now part of Sogrape, producing both port and sherry from Jerez.

milestones.event: Sandeman founded in London by George Sandeman, with Jerez sherry operations established
Royal Esmeralda Fino positioned as the premium Fino in the Sandeman Jerez range

wines.description: Sandeman Jerez premium dry Fino, light and elegant with almonds, sea minerals and a clean refreshing finish.

wines.name: Royal Ambrosante Pedro Ximénez VOS
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; VOS certified minimum 20 years average age; oxidative ageing

pairings.why: VOS PX's concentrated dark-fruit and coffee character is a natural companion to deep chocolate baking.
Classic PX service: the viscous sweetness and cold dairy produce the quintessential Jerez dessert contrast.
Extreme PX sweetness provides dramatic contrast to the salt and pungency of blue cheese.

history.summary: Royal Ambrosante is Sandeman Jerez's prestige Pedro Ximénez, a VOS-certified wine of minimum 20 years average age from old soleras in the Jerez triangle. The name ambrosante derives from ambrosia, the food of the gods, reflecting the wine's rich and intensely sweet character.

milestones.event: Royal Ambrosante PX solera established at Sandeman Jerez
VOS certification conferred by the Consejo Regulador

wines.description: Sandeman Jerez VOS Pedro Ximénez, minimum 20 years average age. Near-black, intensely sweet with concentrated raisins, molasses and a very long finish.

wines.name: Royal Corregidor Rich Old Oloroso VOS
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; VOS certified minimum 20 years average age; Oloroso sweetened with old PX

pairings.why: The wine's toffee sweetness and vanilla warmth mirror the caramelised character of bread pudding.
Nutty-sweet notes in the wine are amplified by roasted walnuts and dried fruit.
The soft, washed-rind cheese's creaminess is lifted by the wine's sweet-spice character.

history.summary: Royal Corregidor takes its name from the Corregidor, the Royal Administrator in the Jerez region during the Spanish Golden Age. Sandeman Jerez has produced this sweet Oloroso VOS from very old soleras since the early 20th century, sweetening the base Oloroso with Pedro Ximénez to the medium-sweet profile favoured in the British market.

milestones.event: Royal Corregidor Oloroso solera established at Sandeman Jerez
VOS certification conferred by the Consejo Regulador

wines.description: Sandeman Jerez VOS sweet Oloroso, minimum 20 years average age. Caramel, toffee, dried orange and mocha with a long warming finish.

wines.name: Character Amontillado
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; dual biological and oxidative solera; Character range; average 6-8 years

pairings.dish: Tapas of croquetas and patatas bravas
pairings.why: The approachable dry-nuttiness of Character Amontillado pairs well with Spanish bar food.
Moderate complexity of commercial Amontillado suits mild cheese platters for everyday enjoyment.
The wine's almond register and moderate sweetness complement these classic accompaniments.

history.summary: Character Amontillado is the entry-level dry style in Sandeman Jerez's Character range, designed for everyday accessibility. The wine is produced from the standard Sandeman solera-criadera system in Jerez and is the house's main commercial Amontillado, widely distributed in major markets.

milestones.event: Character Amontillado introduced in the Sandeman Jerez Character range

wines.description: Sandeman Jerez entry-level Amontillado from the Character range. Toasted hazelnuts, light toffee and a dry hazelnut finish.

wines.name: La Ina Fino
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; biological ageing under flor; historic Pedro Domecq solera now operated by Beam Suntory; average 4-5 years

pairings.why: The classic Spanish aperitivo pairing of dry Fino and cured ham.
Light body and salinity pair well with garlic prawns.
Classic bar snacks that amplify the wine's own olive-brine and almond notes.

history.summary: La Ina Fino was the flagship Fino product of Pedro Domecq, founded in 1730. The La Ina brand was established in the late 18th century. Following the dissolution of the Pedro Domecq company, the brand is now held by Beam Suntory.

milestones.event: La Ina Fino brand established by Pedro Domecq
Pedro Domecq assets, including La Ina, acquired by Allied Domecq and subsequently Beam Suntory

wines.description: La Ina Fino from the historic Pedro Domecq house, now under Beam Suntory. Dry, light and clean with fresh almonds and a saline finish.

wines.name: Sibarita Oloroso VORS
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; VORS certified minimum 30 years average age; fully oxidative ageing from the historic Pedro Domecq solera

pairings.why: The wine's extraordinary age and full oxidative depth match the intensity of slow-cooked game.
Very old Oloroso is a natural companion to aged hard Spanish cheese with sweet quince paste.
The tobacco and espresso character of very old Oloroso amplifies fine bitter chocolate.

history.summary: The Sibarita VORS Oloroso is drawn from soleras at the historic Pedro Domecq cellars that date to the late 19th century. The Sibarita brand name has been maintained through the transitions from Pedro Domecq to Allied Domecq to Beam Suntory.

milestones.event: Sibarita Oloroso solera established at Pedro Domecq
VORS certification conferred by the Consejo Regulador

wines.description: Pedro Domecq VORS Oloroso from soleras dating to 1870, minimum 30 years average age. Very old leather, tobacco, espresso and an extraordinarily long dry finish.

wines.name: Classic Fino
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; biological ageing under flor; Classic range; average 3-4 years

pairings.why: Classic Spanish potato omelette is a natural everyday companion to a fresh dry Fino.
Dry Fino is the universal companion to Spanish cured meats and cheese.
The high acidity and lightness of Fino cut through the batter of fried squid.

history.summary: The Classic range of Rey Fernando de Castilla was established to provide accessible, everyday styles at the Santiago-quarter bodega in Jerez. The Classic Fino is the entry-level biological sherry in the range, produced from the same Santiago solera infrastructure as the premium Antique series.

milestones.event: Classic Fino introduced in the Rey Fernando de Castilla Classic range

wines.description: Rey Fernando de Castilla Classic Fino, dry and light with green apple, almonds and a clean high-acid finish from biological ageing.

verified.address_quoted: C/Jardinillo, 7 al 11, Jerez de la Frontera-11404, Cádiz. SPAIN

wines.name: Classic Amontillado
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; dual biological and oxidative solera; Classic range; average 6-8 years

pairings.why: The nutty character of Classic Amontillado pairs with ham croquettes for a classic tapa combination.
Roast chicken with Andalusian herbs is a natural everyday companion to an approachable Amontillado.
The mild nutty complexity of Classic Amontillado matches medium-aged Manchego.

history.summary: Classic Amontillado is the accessible, everyday Amontillado in the Rey Fernando de Castilla Classic range. It represents the style of traditional Jerez Amontillado available at an accessible price, positioned below the premium Antique Amontillado from the same bodega.

milestones.event: Classic Amontillado introduced in the Rey Fernando de Castilla Classic range

wines.description: Rey Fernando de Castilla Classic Amontillado, approachable with hazelnuts, light toffee and a dry nutty finish.

verified.address_quoted: C/Jardinillo, 7 al 11, Jerez de la Frontera-11404, Cádiz. SPAIN

wines.name: Tío Mateo Fino
wines.producer_name: Grupo Estévez (Real Tesoro / Marqués del Real Tesoro)
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; biological ageing under flor; Grupo Estévez Fino solera operated under the Real Tesoro brand; average 4-5 years

pairings.why: The classic dry Fino and cured ham pairing.
The wine's brine character amplifies the saltiness of cured olives.
Small fried anchovies pair naturally with the acid freshness of a dry Fino.

history.summary: Tío Mateo Fino was developed as the everyday Fino label at Real Tesoro, the house owned by Grupo Estévez in Jerez. The Tío Mateo name is a traditional family reference; the wine represents an accessible, widely-distributed Fino from the same Jerez infrastructure that produces the more premium Inocente single-vineyard Fino.

milestones.event: Tío Mateo Fino established in the Real Tesoro commercial range
Grupo Estévez acquires Real Tesoro and Valdespino, maintaining the Tío Mateo brand

wines.description: Grupo Estévez Tío Mateo Fino from Real Tesoro, accessible and dry with fresh almonds, sea salt and a clean refreshing finish.

verified.address_quoted: Jerez de la Frontera, Cádiz, Spain

wines.name: Fino Dios Baco
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; biological ageing under flor; average 4-5 years

pairings.why: Classic Spanish aperitivo pairing that matches the wine's almond and mineral character.
Dry Fino's acidity cuts through fried seafood naturally.
The wine's saline brine echoes the olive brine in the classic bar snack.

history.summary: Bodegas Dios Baco was founded in 1848 with a focus on traditional Jerez sherry styles. The Fino Dios Baco is produced from Palomino Fino from albariza soils aged under flor in American oak butts through the traditional solera-criadera system, maintaining the classic biological sherry style.

milestones.event: Fino Dios Baco established in the house's commercial range

wines.description: Bodegas Dios Baco traditional Jerez Fino. Dry and light with almonds, citrus minerals and a clean high-acid finish.

verified.address_quoted: Jerez de la Frontera, Cádiz, Spain

wines.name: Oxford Dry Oloroso
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; fully oxidative solera; average 8-10 years

pairings.why: The full oxidative depth of Oloroso pairs with the rich Andalusian pork tradition.
Classic Spanish aged cheese pairing with dry Oloroso.
Roasted walnut notes in the Oloroso amplify the character of smoked almonds.

history.summary: Oxford Dry Oloroso is named after the historic British university town, reflecting the long tradition of the British sherry trade. Bodegas Dios Baco developed this fully dry Oloroso for export to the UK market, where dry styles were preferred by the educated consumer rather than the standard sweet cream.

milestones.event: Oxford Dry Oloroso established in the Dios Baco range for UK export

wines.description: Bodegas Dios Baco Oxford Dry Oloroso from a fully oxidative solera. Walnuts, coffee and dried fruit with a long dry warming finish.

verified.address_quoted: Jerez de la Frontera, Cádiz, Spain

wines.name: Pedro Ximénez Dios Baco
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; oxidative solera; average 8 years

pairings.why: The wine's dark chocolate and raisin notes amplify rich chocolate ice cream.
Dried fig and nut notes in the PX mirror this traditional cake combination.
The extreme sweetness provides dramatic contrast to the pungent blue cheese.

history.summary: Pedro Ximénez Dios Baco is produced from sun-dried PX grapes whose sugars concentrate on esparto mats before pressing and fermentation. The wine is aged through an oxidative solera in American oak at the historic Dios Baco bodega in Jerez, producing a rich and intensely sweet dessert sherry.

milestones.event: Pedro Ximénez solera established at Bodegas Dios Baco

wines.description: Bodegas Dios Baco PX from an oxidative Jerez solera. Intensely sweet with raisins, toffee and dark chocolate.

verified.address_quoted: Jerez de la Frontera, Cádiz, Spain

wines.name: Fino Urium
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; biological ageing under flor from small artisan solera at Bodegas Urium; average 4-5 years

pairings.dish: Coquinas al ajillo (wedge clams with garlic)
pairings.why: The wine's saline freshness and mineral depth complement small clams with garlic in the Andalusian style.
Small-production Fino brings out the subtle sweetness of acorn-fed Iberian ham.
Classic bar snacks that mirror the wine's own almond and brine character.

history.summary: Bodegas Urium was founded in 1986 as a small artisan Jerez producer committed to traditional methods. The Fino is produced from old American oak butts and aged under flor in the traditional solera-criadera method without industrial-scale manipulation. Limited production makes these wines available primarily through specialist merchants.

milestones.event: Fino Urium released in the first commercial bottlings of the new artisan bodega

wines.description: Artisan Bodegas Urium Fino from small Jerez soleras. Dry and clean with almonds, beeswax and a long saline finish.

wines.name: Oloroso Urium
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; fully oxidative solera at Bodegas Urium; average 10-12 years

pairings.why: The full oxidative body of Urium Oloroso matches the rich intensity of braised tail.
Dry, complex Oloroso pairs naturally with aged sheep's or goat's cheese.
Coffee and walnut notes in the wine echo quality dark chocolate confections.

history.summary: Urium's Oloroso is a fully oxidative wine from the small artisan solera established when the bodega was founded. Like all Urium wines, production is limited and the wine is bottled without industrial filtration, reflecting the traditional approach of the independent bodega.

milestones.event: Oloroso Urium introduced in the artisan bodega's range

wines.description: Bodegas Urium artisan dry Oloroso. Full and warming with roasted walnuts, dark fruit and espresso from a small fully oxidative solera.

wines.name: Pedro Ximénez Urium
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; oxidative solera at Bodegas Urium; average 8 years

pairings.why: Classic Jerez PX-over-ice-cream service.
Dark raisin and caramel notes of PX amplify a rich chocolate cake.
PX sweetness provides classic contrast with pungent blue-veined cheeses.

history.summary: Urium's Pedro Ximénez is made from sun-dried PX grapes, concentrated on mats before pressing and fermentation. The small artisan bodega ages the wine through an oxidative solera in old American oak butts, maintaining the traditional Jerez approach to PX production in limited quantities.

milestones.event: Pedro Ximénez Urium released in the artisan bodega's range

wines.description: Bodegas Urium artisan PX. Rich and sweet with concentrated raisins, dark caramel and a long viscous finish.

wines.name: Amontillado La Cave Morales
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; dual biological and oxidative ageing from the small La Cave Morales solera; average 8-10 years

pairings.why: The nutty, savoury Amontillado pairs naturally with fried squid in the traditional Spanish sandwich.
The dry complexity of Amontillado enhances the saltiness of cured meats and olives.
Dry, nutty Amontillado alongside cold tomato-pepper soup is a traditional Andalusian pairing.

history.summary: La Cave Morales is a small family producer in the historic centre of Jerez whose soleras have been maintained since the late 19th century. The Amontillado is made from Palomino Fino that begins as a Fino under biological ageing and transitions naturally to oxidative ageing as the flor diminishes.

milestones.event: La Cave Morales Amontillado solera established in the Jerez historic centre

wines.description: La Cave Morales small-production Amontillado from Jerez's historic centre. Hazelnuts, toffee and a dry nutty finish.

wines.name: Oloroso La Cave Morales
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; fully oxidative solera; average 8-10 years

pairings.why: The full-bodied Oloroso pairs with this Andalusian pork braise's concentrated tomato sauce.
The oxidative depth and full body complement cured Iberian sausage.
Dry Oloroso is a natural companion to hard aged Spanish cheeses.

history.summary: La Cave Morales Oloroso is produced from Palomino Fino fortified to Oloroso levels without flor contact, aged through a fully oxidative solera in the small family bodega in Jerez's historic centre. The wine is available primarily through Lustau's Almacenista programme and specialist importers.

milestones.event: La Cave Morales Oloroso solera established in the Jerez historic centre

wines.description: La Cave Morales small-production Oloroso from Jerez. Full and dry with walnuts, coffee and a long warming finish.

wines.name: Amontillado de la Riva
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; dual biological and oxidative solera from the 18th-century De la Riva cellar; average 12-15 years

pairings.why: The wine's complex nutty oxidative character and medium body complement dry-cured Iberian products.
Dried-fruit and caramel notes bridge the gamey richness of liver pâté.
Aged sheep's cheese is a natural peer for old-solera Amontillado.

history.summary: Bodegas de la Riva was founded in 1776 as an almacenista house, aging wines in private cellars for eventual sale to larger bodegas. The Amontillado solera has been maintained through the 19th and 20th centuries and now contributes to Lustau's Almacenista programme, which bottles single-source wines from named almacenistas without blending.

milestones.event: Bodegas de la Riva founded as an almacenista house in Jerez
De la Riva Amontillado included in Lustau's inaugural Almacenista programme

wines.description: De la Riva almacenista Amontillado from 18th-century Jerez soleras. Roasted hazelnuts, beeswax and a long complex dry finish.

wines.name: Oloroso Viejo de la Riva
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; fully oxidative old solera at Bodegas de la Riva; average 15-20 years

pairings.why: The wine's full-bodied depth and old wood notes match the intensity of game meat slow-cooked with aromatics.
The pungent creamy sheep's milk cheese from Extremadura is well matched by very old Oloroso.
Old dry Oloroso, hard cheese and quince paste is a classic Andalusian combination.

history.summary: De la Riva's Oloroso Viejo is the old Oloroso from the almacenista house's historic cellar, maintained in American oak butts from the early 19th century. The wine has been drawn for supply to the Lustau Almacenista programme as well as direct export to specialist importers who seek out old-solera almacenista wines.

milestones.event: Old Oloroso solera established at Bodegas de la Riva
De la Riva Oloroso featured in Lustau's Almacenista launch

wines.description: De la Riva almacenista old Oloroso from 19th-century Jerez soleras. Deep and dry with walnut, tobacco and a long warming finish.

wines.name: Tío Mateo Fino
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; biological ageing under flor in the Jerez solera system operated under Estévez group; average 4-5 years

pairings.why: An accessible everyday Fino pairs naturally with all manner of Spanish bar tapas.
The classic aperitivo combination of dry Fino with cured ham and nuts.
Simple Andalusian potato dish with green peppers and olive oil pairs with light dry Fino.

history.summary: Palomino & Vergara was founded in 1765 as one of the old Jerez trading houses and is now part of the Estévez group. The Tío Mateo Fino has been the house's accessible everyday Fino label since the late 19th century, maintaining continuous production through various ownership transitions.

milestones.event: Palomino & Vergara founding house established in Jerez
Tío Mateo Fino established in the Palomino & Vergara commercial range

wines.description: Palomino & Vergara Tío Mateo Fino, accessible and dry with fresh almonds, chamomile and a clean refreshing finish.

wines.name: Manzanilla Medina
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; biological ageing under flor in the Sanlúcar solera; average 3-4 years

pairings.why: The local pairing: Sanlúcar prawns with Sanlúcar Manzanilla.
Delicate Manzanilla's acidity and salinity complement fried small fish without overwhelming them.
Saline brine character of the wine is amplified by marinated olives.

history.summary: Bodegas Medina was founded in 1911 in Sanlúcar de Barrameda, where the proximity to the Atlantic and the Guadalquivir estuary produces the unique conditions for Manzanilla ageing. The Manzanilla Medina is a traditional production from old flor-covered soleras in the Sanlúcar coastal climate.

milestones.event: Manzanilla Medina introduced in the family bodega's commercial range

wines.description: Bodegas Medina Manzanilla from Sanlúcar de Barrameda. Delicate, saline and dry with camomile and lemon zest.

wines.name: Moscatel Medina
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; oxidative solera from Moscatel de Alejandría grapes; average 6-8 years

pairings.why: The floral, light sweetness of Moscatel enhances fresh tropical or summer fruits.
Traditional Andalusian almond shortbread is naturally complemented by aromatic Moscatel.
The honey sweetness of Moscatel mirrors the egg custard and pastry of tarts.

history.summary: Bodegas Medina produces Moscatel from Moscatel de Alejandría grapes grown in the sandy soils near Sanlúcar and Chipiona, where this grape variety has historically thrived. Moscatel sherry is a distinct style from Pedro Ximénez: lighter in body, more floral and aromatic, and less intensely concentrated.

milestones.event: Moscatel Medina introduced in the Sanlúcar bodega's range

wines.description: Bodegas Medina Moscatel from Sanlúcar's sandy soils. Floral with orange blossom, dried apricot and honey, lighter than Pedro Ximénez.

wines.name: Fino Jerezano Soto
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; biological ageing under flor in the historic Soto cellar; average 4-5 years

pairings.why: The light, dry Fino pairs with the local Cadiz specialty of shrimp fritters.
Dry Fino and Iberian ham is the most classic of all Andalusian pairings.
The herbal Fino notes complement the earthy spices of Sevillano spinach-and-chickpeas.

history.summary: Bodegas José de Soto was founded in 1765 and is among the oldest surviving family bodegas in Jerez. The Fino Jerezano has been produced from the house's soleras since the early 19th century, maintaining the classic biological Fino style through multiple family generations.

milestones.event: Bodegas José de Soto founded in Jerez
Fino Jerezano established in the house's commercial range

wines.description: José de Soto Fino from one of Jerez's oldest family bodegas, founded 1765. Dry and light with almonds, sea minerals and a clean finish.

wines.name: Oloroso Viejo Soto
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; fully oxidative old solera at Bodegas José de Soto; average 15+ years

pairings.why: The wine's full-bodied oxidative depth and warm complexity match the rich, sweet lamb.
Very old Oloroso and hard aged sheep's cheese is a classic Castilian-Andalusian combination.
Walnut notes in the wine are naturally amplified by roasted walnuts and sweet dried figs.

history.summary: José de Soto's old Oloroso is produced from fully oxidative soleras dating to the early 19th century, maintained through successive generations of the Soto family in the historic centre of Jerez. The wine represents the old-style Oloroso that was once the most common premium sherry style before Amontillado and Palo Cortado gained prominence.

milestones.event: Old Oloroso solera established at Bodegas José de Soto

wines.description: José de Soto old Oloroso from soleras dating to the early 19th century. Deep and dry with walnuts, tobacco and a long finish.

wines.name: Fino Páez Morilla
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; biological ageing under flor in the traditional Páez Morilla solera; average 4-5 years

pairings.why: Classic Spanish aperitivo combination naturally matched by dry Fino.

pairings.why: Light acidity and low body complement fried coastal fish without competing.
The wine's brine character is echoed by marinated Andalusian olives.

history.summary: Bodegas Páez Morilla was founded in 1870 and has maintained traditional Fino and Pedro Ximénez production from old soleras in the Jerez historic centre. The family bodega operates on a small scale typical of almacenista houses, contributing aged wines to the regional network.

milestones.event: Fino Páez Morilla established in the family bodega's commercial range

wines.description: Bodegas Páez Morilla small-production Fino from Jerez. Dry and light with almonds, saline minerals and a clean finish.

wines.name: Pedro Ximénez Páez Morilla
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; oxidative solera; average 8 years

pairings.why: Classic PX pairing: drizzled over cold vanilla creates the archetypal Jerez dessert.
The sweet raisin and chocolate notes amplify dark chocolate baking.
PX sweetness provides contrast to pungent blue-veined cheese.

history.summary: Páez Morilla's Pedro Ximénez is produced from sun-dried PX grapes aged through an oxidative solera in the small Jerez family bodega. The wine represents the classic small-scale almacenista PX that was historically common across the Jerez triangle before consolidation into larger houses.

milestones.event: Pedro Ximénez solera established at Bodegas Páez Morilla

wines.description: Bodegas Páez Morilla small-production PX from old Jerez soleras. Rich and sweet with concentrated raisins, toffee and dark chocolate.

wines.name: Amontillado Faustino González
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; dual biological and oxidative ageing; average 8-10 years

pairings.why: Nutty Amontillado pairs well with the savoury richness of salt cod and béchamel in fried croquettes.
The earthy, savoury notes of Amontillado complement garlic-sautéed mushrooms.
Medium-aged sheep's cheese with the nutty moderate complexity of family Amontillado.

history.summary: Bodegas Faustino González was founded in 1920 as a mid-sized family Jerez producer contributing Amontillado and Oloroso to the regional almacenista network. The Amontillado is produced from traditional dual biological and oxidative soleras in Jerez's historic production zone.

milestones.event: Amontillado Faustino González established in the family bodega's commercial range

wines.description: Bodegas Faustino González traditional Amontillado from mid-sized family Jerez soleras. Hazelnuts, toffee and a dry nutty finish.

wines.name: Oloroso Faustino González
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; fully oxidative solera; average 10 years

pairings.why: The full-bodied Oloroso pairs with the richness of this traditional Cádiz chickpea stew with meats.
Dry Oloroso and aged Spanish cheese is a natural pairing combination.
The wine's roasted nut notes are amplified by smoked almonds or walnuts.

history.summary: Oloroso Faustino González is produced from Palomino Fino fortified to Oloroso levels and aged under fully oxidative conditions in American oak butts at the Jerez family bodega. The wine is available through the almacenista network and specialist export channels.

milestones.event: Oloroso Faustino González established in the family bodega's range

wines.description: Bodegas Faustino González dry Oloroso from a fully oxidative family solera. Roasted nuts, coffee and dried fruit with a long warming finish.

wines.name: Jarana Manzanilla
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; biological ageing under flor in Sanlúcar; average 3-4 years

pairings.why: The wine's marine salinity and sharp acidity complement marinated anchovies.
Atlantic salinity and racy acidity match fresh clams cooked with white wine and garlic.
Classic dry Manzanilla and cured ham pairing.

history.summary: Bodegas García Jarana was founded in 1930 in Sanlúcar de Barrameda, where the proximity to the Guadalquivir estuary and the Atlantic Ocean produces the unique Atlantic-influenced conditions for biological ageing of Manzanilla. The Jarana Manzanilla is the house's flagship bottling from traditional flor-aged soleras.

milestones.event: Jarana Manzanilla established in Bodegas García Jarana's commercial range

wines.description: Bodegas García Jarana Manzanilla from Sanlúcar's Atlantic-influenced soleras. Delicate, saline and dry with chamomile and lemon zest.

wines.name: Imperial VORS Oloroso
wines.oak_regime: American oak butts; VORS certified minimum 30 years average age; fully oxidative ageing

pairings.dish: Iberian cured black leg ham (pata negra)
pairings.why: The age and complexity of VORS Oloroso pairs with premium Iberian charcuterie.
Coffee and dark-fruit notes of very old Oloroso echo fine dark chocolate.

pairings.dish: Torta del Casar or Torta de la Serena
pairings.why: Very old dry Oloroso pairs with the pungent, runny-textured sheep's milk cheeses of Extremadura.

history.summary: The Imperial VORS Oloroso represents the prestige summit of the Díez-Mérito range, drawn from soleras established in the late 19th century when the house was at its commercial height. VORS certification by the Consejo Regulador confirms minimum 30 years average age through isotopic analysis.

milestones.event: Imperial Oloroso solera established at Díez-Mérito
VORS certification conferred by the Consejo Regulador

wines.description: Díez-Mérito Imperial VORS Oloroso, minimum 30 years average age. Old leather, tobacco, espresso and an extraordinarily long dry finish.

wines.name: Solear Manzanilla
wines.oak_regime: Biological ageing under flor in American oak butts; solera system

taste.summary: Classic Sanlúcar Manzanilla with pronounced saline character from Atlantic coastal ageing; crisp, dry and refreshing.

pairings.why: Salt and sea character mirrors the ocean-fresh sweetness of prawns.
Bright acidity cuts through the fatty richness of cured ham.
Saline minerality complements the batter-fried seafood of Cádiz tradition.

history.summary: Solear is the flagship Manzanilla of Barbadillo, produced continuously since the bodega's founding in 1821. Aged in the Barrio Alto quarter of Sanlúcar de Barrameda under a thick veil of flor yeast in Atlantic-influenced cellars. The Sanlúcar microclimate maintains year-round humidity that sustains thicker flor activity than inland Jerez, producing the characteristic saline-chamomile Manzanilla profile.

milestones.event: Barbadillo founded in Sanlúcar; Manzanilla solera established
Solear label first registered as a commercial brand

wines.description: Barbadillo Solear is the standard-bearer Sanlúcar Manzanilla: light, saline, briny with green-apple freshness shaped by year-round flor ageing at sea level.

wines.name: Solear Manzanilla En Rama
wines.oak_regime: Biological ageing under flor in American oak butts; bottled unfiltered direct from solera

taste.summary: Unfiltered direct-from-solera Manzanilla with richer yeasty texture and more pronounced flor character than the standard Solear; released seasonally.

pairings.why: Intense ocean iodine of the anemones finds a match in the wine's sea-salt register.
Citric acidity and saline finish bridge the acid-cured anchovies.
Clean briny minerality enhances the fresh clam flavour.

history.summary: Barbadillo pioneered the commercial en rama category in the early 2000s when they began bottling Solear directly from the solera with minimal filtration, preserving the live flor yeast contribution. The practice of tapping the solera in spring when flor is thickest became the model for the Sherry region's en rama movement.

milestones.event: Barbadillo launches Solear En Rama as one of the first commercially available en rama bottlings
Seasonal spring release format formalised

wines.description: Unfiltered Manzanilla bottled direct from the Solear solera, releasing live flor complexity; saline, doughy and richer than the standard filtered release.

wines.name: Príncipe Amontillado
wines.oak_regime: Progressive ageing: biological under flor, then oxidative in American oak butts; solera system

taste.summary: Dry Amontillado from Sanlúcar with the characteristic saline edge of its origin; hazelnut and orange peel aromatics over a medium-bodied, clean palate.

pairings.why: Nutty oxidative notes complement the crystalline, aged cheese texture.
Dry Amontillado is a classic pairing with bouillon; the wine's umami depth mirrors the stock.
Toasted-nut palate aligns with warm almonds and the salt of cured meats.

history.summary: Príncipe is one of Barbadillo's core aged styles, an Amontillado produced under the Sanlúcar classification rather than the standard Jerez DO. The wine begins life as Manzanilla under flor, then transitions to oxidative ageing once the flor dies back, gaining walnut and caramel depth while retaining the Atlantic saline edge.

milestones.event: Barbadillo establishes Manzanilla-to-Amontillado solera progression
Príncipe registered as standalone commercial label

wines.description: Dry Amontillado from Sanlúcar: hazelnut and dried orange peel with the saline Atlantic edge characteristic of the Manzanilla terroir.

wines.name: Cuco Oloroso Seco
wines.oak_regime: Oxidative ageing in American oak butts; no biological phase; solera system

taste.summary: Dry Oloroso with full, warm body; dried fig and walnut core over a rich rancio finish characteristic of long oxidative solera ageing.

pairings.why: The wine's full oxidative richness stands up to slow-braised collagen-rich beef.
Intense salty creaminess of blue cheese finds balance in the dry, nutty Oloroso.
Gamey depth and rancio complexity align in both wine and dish.

history.summary: Cuco is Barbadillo's dry Oloroso from Sanlúcar, aged without biological flor coverage from the outset. The Oloroso category takes its name from the Spanish for 'fragrant': the wine's oxidative development under the Sanlúcar microclimate produces the characteristically complex dried-fruit and rancio profile of aged solera wine.

milestones.event: Oxidative Oloroso solera established at Barbadillo
Cuco brand name registered

wines.description: Dry Sanlúcar Oloroso from Barbadillo: full-bodied, oxidative, with dried fig, walnut and rancio character from old American oak soleras.

wines.name: Eva Cream
wines.oak_regime: Oloroso base aged in American oak butts, sweetened with Pedro Ximénez; solera system

taste.summary: Cream sherry blending Oloroso base with Pedro Ximénez for a medium-sweet, caramel-toned, approachable style.

pairings.why: The cream's sweet caramel notes echo the burnt-sugar crust of the dessert.
Dried-fruit sweetness in the wine matches candied peel and sultanas in the cake.
Sweet-savoury tension is a classic cream sherry pairing.

history.summary: Eva Cream is Barbadillo's heritage cream sherry, named after a traditional bodega brand. The Cream style was developed in the 19th century for export to northern European markets seeking sweeter fortified wines. It blends an Oloroso Seco base from the Sanlúcar soleras with concentrated Pedro Ximénez to reach a medium-sweet balance.

milestones.event: Eva Cream label established for export markets

wines.description: Barbadillo's heritage Cream sherry: Oloroso base sweetened with PX, delivering caramel, dried apricot and hazelnut notes.

wines.name: La Cilla Pedro Ximénez
wines.oak_regime: Oxidative ageing in American oak butts from sun-dried Pedro Ximénez must; solera system

taste.summary: Rich, concentrated Pedro Ximénez made from sun-dried grapes; intensely sweet with coffee, molasses and dried-fruit complexity.

pairings.why: Classic PX service: the wine's molasses intensity contrasts cold vanilla cream.
Bitter chocolate and the wine's coffee register unite seamlessly.
Intense sweetness cuts through pungent blue cheese.

history.summary: La Cilla PX is produced from Pedro Ximénez grapes dried on esparto mats under the Sanlúcar sun, concentrating sugars to extreme levels before pressing. The must ferments partially, then fortification arrests fermentation, retaining natural residual sugar. Aged in American oak solera, the wine darkens to near-black with age.

milestones.event: PX solera established at Barbadillo
La Cilla label registered as standalone PX

wines.description: Barbadillo La Cilla: rich, near-black Pedro Ximénez from sun-dried grapes, delivering molasses, coffee and dried-fruit intensity from old Sanlúcar soleras.

wines.name: San Rafael Oloroso
wines.oak_regime: Long oxidative ageing in American oak butts; VOS solera

taste.summary: Aged Oloroso of at least 20 years from a VOS solera; concentrated dried-fruit and rancio character with notable complexity from extended oxidative ageing.

pairings.why: Umami-rich braised pork finds a mirror in the wine's savoury rancio depth.
Crystalline, nutty aged cheese amplifies the wine's walnut core.
Earthy, savoury umami of wild mushrooms aligns with the wine's oxidative character.

history.summary: San Rafael is Barbadillo's VOS (Vinum Optimum Signatum, minimum 20 years average age) Oloroso, named for the patron saint of travellers. These aged solera wines were historically maintained for religious ceremonies and notable events at the bodega. The wine represents the upper tier of Barbadillo's oxidative range before the Reliquia solera.

milestones.event: San Rafael VOS Oloroso solera established at Barbadillo

wines.description: Barbadillo San Rafael VOS Oloroso: minimum 20-year average age, concentrated dried-fruit and rancio complexity from old Sanlúcar soleras.

wines.name: Versos 1891 Amontillado
wines.oak_regime: Extended biological phase under flor, then oxidative; VORS solera (average over 30 years)

taste.summary: VORS Amontillado from a solera begun in 1891; the extended Sanlúcar biological phase gives uncommon saline freshness to the otherwise oxidative profile.

pairings.why: Umami from both bird and fungi is elevated by the wine's hazelnut and saline depth.
Nutty Swiss-style cheese mirrors the walnut oil in the Amontillado.
Classic VORS Amontillado service with concentrated savoury stock.

history.summary: Versos 1891 takes its name from the year the founding solera butts were laid down by the Barbadillo family. A VORS wine (Vinum Optimum Rare Signatum, average age over 30 years), the Amontillado retains the Sanlúcar saline terroir even after decades of oxidative development because the biological phase was prolonged before flor died back.

milestones.event: Solera foundation butts laid down at Barbadillo's Sanlúcar bodega
Versos 1891 label released commercially

wines.description: VORS Amontillado from a solera begun in 1891; over 30 years average age with hazelnut, saline and dried-apricot complexity from Sanlúcar's unique coastal microclimate.

wines.name: Obispo Gascón Palo Cortado
wines.oak_regime: Rare biological-to-oxidative transition; intermediate solera combining Amontillado and Oloroso characteristics

taste.finish: long with characteristic Palo Cortado tension between biological and oxidative
taste.summary: Palo Cortado from Sanlúcar with the hazelnut nose of Amontillado and the full, rancio palate of Oloroso; the intermediate style at its most distinct.

pairings.dish: Cured ham with aged cheese board
pairings.why: The wine's dual personality bridges the salty-dry and the nutty-rich.
Rich game fat requires the wine's combined acidity and oxidative weight.
Oily fish with charred skin echoes the wine's dried-orange and saline notes.

history.summary: Palo Cortado is the rarest and most unpredictable sherry style, arising when a cask destined for Fino or Manzanilla biological ageing spontaneously loses its flor coverage partway through development. Barbadillo's Obispo Gascón, named after a 19th-century Bishop of Cádiz, is a consistently bottled Palo Cortado from the Sanlúcar designation, offering the distinctive Amontillado-Oloroso hybrid profile.

milestones.event: Obispo Gascón Palo Cortado designation established at Barbadillo

wines.description: Barbadillo Obispo Gascón: a Sanlúcar Palo Cortado blending Amontillado aromatic finesse with Oloroso body depth; rare and distinctive.

wines.name: Reliquia Amontillado
wines.oak_regime: VORS solera, estimated average age over 100 years; American oak butts

taste.summary: Century-old solera Amontillado of extreme concentration; the rancio and mineral depth reflect decades of slow oxidative transformation in old American oak.

pairings.dish: Sip alone or with dark chocolate
pairings.why: At this level of complexity, the wine is best appreciated with minimal food competition.
Crystalline umami of extreme-aged cheese matches the wine's concentrated mineral depth.

history.summary: The Reliquia series at Barbadillo draws from soleras believed to contain wine from the late 18th century. These are among the oldest continuously maintained solera wines in the Sanlúcar DO. Released in tiny quantities, the Reliquia range represents the bodega's deepest archive of oxidative and biological ageing development.

milestones.event: Foundation of the oldest surviving Reliquia solera butts
Reliquia series first released commercially in limited quantities

wines.description: Barbadillo Reliquia: century-old solera Amontillado of exceptional rarity and concentration, among the oldest continuously maintained sherry wines in Sanlúcar.

wines.name: La Gitana Manzanilla
wines.oak_regime: Biological ageing under flor in American oak butts; solera system from Miraflores pago

taste.summary: Widely distributed Manzanilla from Hidalgo La Gitana; delicate and saline with chamomile aromatics and a refreshingly briny finish from Atlantic Sanlúcar ageing.

pairings.why: Delicate crustacean sweetness is heightened by the wine's saline minerality.
Salty, nutty Iberian ham finds its canonical partner in cold, dry Manzanilla.
Citric acidity and brininess echo the lime-cured fish.

history.summary: Bodegas Hidalgo founded in 1792 by the Hidalgo family; La Gitana became the bodega's flagship Manzanilla label in the 20th century. The wine is produced exclusively from grapes grown in the Miraflores pago above Sanlúcar, where Atlantic winds promote year-round flor growth. The brand name La Gitana (The Gypsy) was registered in the early 20th century and is a widely distributed Manzanilla label.

milestones.event: Bodegas Hidalgo established in Sanlúcar de Barrameda
La Gitana brand name registered commercially

wines.description: La Gitana Manzanilla from Bodegas Hidalgo: delicate, saline and refreshing with chamomile aromatics from Sanlúcar's Miraflores pago.

verified.address_quoted: Calle Banda Playa 42, Sanlúcar de Barrameda

wines.name: Pastrana Manzanilla Pasada
wines.oak_regime: Extended biological ageing under flor followed by partial flor die-back; single-vineyard Pago Miraflores; solera system

taste.summary: Single-vineyard Manzanilla Pasada from the Pago Miraflores: extended biological ageing produces a wine midway between fresh Manzanilla and Amontillado, with uncommon depth and saline persistence.

pairings.why: The wine's saline complexity and slight oxidative depth stand up to the intense, aromatic fish.
Classic Sanlúcar tapas companions for this aged Manzanilla style.
Intense umami from salt-cured fish is balanced by the wine's long, refreshing finish.

history.summary: Pastrana is one of the few single-vineyard Manzanilla Pasadas bottled under a dedicated label. The Pago Miraflores vineyard sits on high-chalky albariza soil above Sanlúcar, exposed to Atlantic breezes that maintain unusually consistent flor. The wine is aged longer than a standard Manzanilla until the flor partially recedes, developing the characteristic intermediate Pasada profile.

milestones.event: Miraflores pago established as source for Hidalgo's Manzanilla production
Pastrana launched as a named single-pago Manzanilla Pasada

wines.description: Pastrana is a single-vineyard Manzanilla Pasada from the Pago Miraflores; extended under-flor ageing gives almond, sea-salt and light rancio complexity beyond standard Manzanilla.

verified.address_quoted: Calle Banda Playa 42, Sanlúcar de Barrameda

wines.name: Napoleón Amontillado
wines.oak_regime: Biological then oxidative ageing in American oak solera; Sanlúcar appellation

taste.finish: long, dry and nutty with persistent saline edge
taste.summary: Sanlúcar Amontillado retaining the Atlantic saline character of the Manzanilla base even after full biological-to-oxidative progression; dry, nutty and fresh.

pairings.why: Nutty saline character of both wine and cheese reinforce each other.
Dry Amontillado's savouriness bridges the herbed roasting juices.
Earthy umami is elevated by the wine's oxidative depth.

history.summary: The Napoleón label at Hidalgo is one of the oldest continuously used Amontillado brand names in Sanlúcar, named in honour of Napoleon Bonaparte who reportedly favoured Spanish sherry during his campaigns. The wine begins as Manzanilla and undergoes a second oxidative ageing phase once flor dies naturally.

milestones.event: Napoleón brand reportedly named in reference to the period of Napoleonic interest in Cádiz sherries
Napoleón Amontillado registered as a commercial label by Hidalgo

wines.description: Hidalgo Napoleón Amontillado: dry, saline-edged Sanlúcar Amontillado with hazelnut aromatics and persistent Atlantic freshness.

verified.address_quoted: Calle Banda Playa 42, Sanlúcar de Barrameda

wines.name: Faraón Oloroso
wines.oak_regime: Oxidative ageing in American oak butts; VORS solera (30+ years average)

taste.summary: VORS Oloroso of 30-plus years average age from Sanlúcar; intensely concentrated with dark dried-fruit, coffee and rancio depth alongside the characteristic saline note of the appellation.

pairings.why: The VORS weight and rancio complexity matches the intensity of long-roasted game.
Assertive blue veining finds an equal in the wine's concentrated savoury depth.
Coffee and chocolate register in both wine and dessert amplify each other.

history.summary: Faraón (Pharaoh) is Hidalgo's VORS Oloroso, representing the deepest tier of the bodega's oxidative range. The name evokes the ancient, near-mythical status of very old sherry soleras. The 30-plus year average age produces extraordinary concentration while retaining the Sanlúcar saline terroir even in an Oloroso style aged without biological influence.

milestones.event: Foundation Oloroso solera established at Hidalgo
Faraón VORS released commercially under its current label

wines.description: Hidalgo Faraón VORS Oloroso: 30-plus year average age, dense with dried fruit, coffee and rancio; saline depth of Sanlúcar persists through the full oxidative weight.

verified.address_quoted: Calle Banda Playa 42, Sanlúcar de Barrameda

wines.name: Triana Pedro Ximénez
wines.oak_regime: Oxidative ageing in American oak butts from sun-dried must; solera system

taste.summary: Classic PX of extreme concentration and sweetness from sun-dried Pedro Ximénez grapes; near-black in colour with molasses, espresso and dark dried-fruit intensity.

pairings.why: Traditional PX service; warm sweet intensity contrasts cold cream.
Almond sponge of the Galician cake resonates with the wine's dried-fruit sweetness.
Sweet-salty tension; the PX sweetness offsets the aggressive funk of the blue.

history.summary: Triana takes its name from the famous Seville neighbourhood historically associated with gypsies, flamenco and the sherry trade. Hidalgo's PX is sourced under the DO Jerez-Xérès-Sherry classification since Pedro Ximénez soleras operate under the Jerez DO even for Sanlúcar bodegas. The grapes are dried on esparto mats in the Montilla area, then transported to Sanlúcar for vinification and ageing.

milestones.event: PX solera established at Hidalgo
Triana PX label registered

wines.description: Hidalgo Triana PX: intensely sweet Pedro Ximénez from sun-dried grapes; near-black with molasses, espresso and prune jam depth.

verified.address_quoted: Calle Banda Playa 42, Sanlúcar de Barrameda

wines.name: La Goya Manzanilla
wines.oak_regime: Biological ageing under flor in American oak butts; solera system established 1744

taste.summary: La Goya is documented as the oldest Manzanilla brand, produced since 1744; fresh, saline and delicate with pronounced flor character from the Barrameda Atlantic microclimate.

pairings.why: Ocean minerality in the wine is a direct match for steamed clams.
The Sanlúcar Manzanilla saline edge is the traditional pairing for top-grade Iberian ham.
Crispy, light batter on squid suits the wine's crisp acidity and sea character.

history.summary: La Goya takes its name from the celebrated 19th-century dancer La Goya who performed in Cádiz. Delgado Zuleta was founded in 1744 in Sanlúcar de Barrameda by the Zuleta family, and La Goya has been produced from the original solera since the bodega's founding.

milestones.event: Delgado Zuleta founded; La Goya solera established in Sanlúcar
La Goya brand name registered commercially

wines.description: La Goya is one of the oldest documented Manzanilla brands, produced at Delgado Zuleta since 1744; light, saline and fresh with green-apple and chamomile character.

verified.address_quoted: Avda. Rocío Jurado, s/n - 11540 Sanlúcar de Barrameda

wines.name: Goya XL Manzanilla En Rama
wines.oak_regime: Biological ageing under flor; bottled unfiltered direct from solera in spring

taste.summary: Unfiltered en rama Manzanilla from Delgado Zuleta; richer in texture and flor-yeast character than the standard La Goya, released in limited spring batches.

pairings.why: Fresh tuna's clean flavour is elevated by the wine's lively acidity and sea-salt register.
The oceanic intensity of barnacles calls for a wine with equal sea-mineral presence.
Smoky umami finds a complement in the doughy, slightly savoury en rama texture.

history.summary: Delgado Zuleta launched Goya XL as part of the growing movement among Sanlúcar bodegas to offer minimally filtered, direct-from-solera Manzanilla. The XL designation refers to the extra time the wine spends in the solera before being drawn off unfiltered in spring when flor activity is at its peak. Released in small numbered batches.

milestones.event: Goya XL En Rama first released by Delgado Zuleta

wines.description: Goya XL is Delgado Zuleta's en rama Manzanilla: bottled unfiltered in spring from the La Goya solera, with richer yeasty texture and brighter flor character.

verified.address_quoted: Avda. Rocío Jurado, s/n - 11540 Sanlúcar de Barrameda

wines.name: Quo Vadis? Amontillado VORS
wines.oak_regime: Extended biological ageing then full oxidative transition; VORS solera (average over 30 years)

taste.summary: VORS Amontillado from a solera maintained since the bodega's founding in 1744; exceptional saline complexity from extended Sanlúcar biological phase and decades of subsequent oxidative development.

pairings.dish: Sip alone or with a small selection of aged cheeses
pairings.why: At VORS age and complexity, the wine merits contemplative service with minimal distraction.
Traditional tapas context for fine aged Amontillado in Cádiz.

history.summary: The name Quo Vadis? (Where are you going?) reflects the profound age and rarity of this VORS Amontillado. The wine draws from solera butts maintained at the Delgado Zuleta bodega since the 18th century. Average age exceeds 30 years, with some butts containing wine considerably older. The saline Atlantic influence of Sanlúcar persists through the extraordinary oxidative development.

milestones.event: Foundation solera established at Delgado Zuleta's Sanlúcar bodega
Quo Vadis? VORS Amontillado released as a named premium tier

wines.description: Delgado Zuleta Quo Vadis? is a VORS Amontillado from one of the oldest continuously maintained soleras in the Sanlúcar denomination; extreme age complexity with saline Atlantic depth.

verified.address_quoted: Avda. Rocío Jurado, s/n - 11540 Sanlúcar de Barrameda

wines.name: Monteagudo Amontillado
wines.oak_regime: Biological then oxidative ageing; mid-tier solera below VORS level

taste.summary: Mid-tier Amontillado from Delgado Zuleta at the accessible entry below VORS; dry, nutty, with the characteristic Sanlúcar saline freshness from the Manzanilla origin.

pairings.why: Classic Andalusian aperitif complement for a dry Amontillado.
Savouriness of grilled shellfish amplifies the wine's mineral salinity.
Rich pâté is cut by the wine's acidity while the nutty body provides depth.

history.summary: Monteagudo is Delgado Zuleta's entry-level Amontillado, positioned below the Quo Vadis? VORS as an accessible expression of the bodega's biological-to-oxidative progression. It captures the characteristic Sanlúcar Amontillado character: saline, fresh and nutty, without the extreme concentration of the VORS tier.

milestones.event: Monteagudo label registered as a commercial Amontillado brand

wines.description: Delgado Zuleta Monteagudo: accessible dry Amontillado from Sanlúcar retaining Atlantic saline freshness; hazelnut, dried herbs and citrus oil character.

verified.address_quoted: Avda. Rocío Jurado, s/n - 11540 Sanlúcar de Barrameda

wines.name: Barbiana Manzanilla Pasada
wines.oak_regime: Extended biological ageing; flor partially recedes; intermediate between Manzanilla and Amontillado

taste.summary: Manzanilla Pasada aged beyond the standard biological phase; the partial flor die-back yields additional complexity and a transition toward Amontillado character while retaining pronounced salinity.

pairings.why: Fine white fish with briny capers echoes the wine's saline, nutty depth.
Marinated fresh anchovies are the regional classic with aged Manzanilla.
Crispy potato omelette with the mineral salinity of a Pasada is a quintessential Andalusian tapa pairing.

history.summary: Barbiana is named after a female figure of Sanlúcar folklore, the companion of La Goya. As a Manzanilla Pasada, it occupies the Delgado Zuleta range between the fresh La Goya and the full Amontillado Monteagudo. The Pasada designation indicates extended ageing in the Manzanilla solera beyond the standard fresh style, with flor beginning to recede naturally.

milestones.event: Barbiana Pasada established as a named intermediate style at Delgado Zuleta

wines.description: Delgado Zuleta Barbiana: Manzanilla Pasada aged past the standard biological phase, gaining almond depth and saline complexity in the transition toward Amontillado.

verified.address_quoted: Avda. Rocío Jurado, s/n - 11540 Sanlúcar de Barrameda

wines.name: San León Manzanilla
wines.oak_regime: Biological ageing under flor in American oak butts; solera system

taste.summary: San León is Argüeso's flagship Manzanilla: light, saline and fresh with chamomile and green apple character from Sanlúcar's Barrio Bajo cellars.

pairings.why: Crisp fried anchovies need the wine's cutting acidity and saline energy.
Fresh tomato-vegetable acidity mirrors the wine's bright profile.
Tiny Sanlúcar clams sautéed in garlic and parsley are the local pairing classic for San León.

history.summary: San León is named after León de Argüeso who founded Bodegas Argüeso in 1822. The cellars are located in the Barrio Bajo quarter of Sanlúcar de Barrameda, directly above the Guadalquivir estuary, where Atlantic humidity maintains continuous flor activity year-round. San León won the Manzanilla Trophy at the International Wine Challenge 2017.

milestones.event: Bodegas Argüeso founded by León de Argüeso in the Barrio Bajo
San León Manzanilla wins the Manzanilla Trophy at the International Wine Challenge

wines.description: San León is Argüeso's flagship Manzanilla from the Barrio Bajo of Sanlúcar: crisp, saline and fresh with chamomile and sea-salt character.

wines.name: San León Reserva de Familia Manzanilla Pasada
wines.oak_regime: Extended biological ageing in American oak butts; flor partially recedes; solera system

taste.summary: The reserve tier of Argüeso's Manzanilla range: aged beyond the standard San León with partial flor die-back, yielding additional almond and mineral complexity while retaining bright salinity.

pairings.dish: Grilled sea bass with lemon butter
pairings.why: The wine's mineral salinity and almond depth elevate delicate grilled fish.
Galician-style octopus with paprika and olive oil is a natural match for aged Manzanilla.
Semi-firm Spanish cheeses with grassy notes pair with the wine's nutty, saline character.

history.summary: The Reserva de Familia is Argüeso's premium Manzanilla Pasada, kept in the family's private solera for longer than the San León standard. The Pasada classification indicates extended under-flor ageing with partial flor recession, producing a wine that bridges the freshness of Manzanilla and the complexity of Amontillado within the Sanlúcar denomination.

milestones.event: Argüeso family soleras established in the Barrio Bajo
Reserva de Familia label created as a limited upper tier

wines.description: Argüeso Reserva de Familia: premium Manzanilla Pasada with extended under-flor ageing; almond, walnut oil and saline complexity from the Barrio Bajo soleras.

wines.name: Las Medallas Amontillado
wines.oak_regime: Biological then oxidative ageing in American oak solera

taste.summary: Sanlúcar Amontillado with hazelnut and toffee character; the biological Manzanilla origin gives a characteristic saline freshness to the oxidative nutty depth.

pairings.why: Thick cold tomato soup finds a savoury counterpart in dry Amontillado.

pairings.dish: Tapas of salted nuts and olives
pairings.why: Classic Andalusian aperitif combination for dry Amontillado.
Rich eggs with earthy truffle benefit from the wine's savoury, mineral depth.

history.summary: Las Medallas (The Medals) takes its name from the show awards Argüeso won in 19th-century European wine expositions. The Amontillado is produced in the Barrio Bajo cellars from a solera that begins as San León Manzanilla and undergoes full biological-to-oxidative progression.

milestones.event: Las Medallas name adopted following Argüeso wins at European exhibitions

wines.description: Argüeso Las Medallas Amontillado: dry Sanlúcar Amontillado with hazelnut, toffee and saline character from a Manzanilla-origin solera.

wines.name: Las Medallas Oloroso
wines.oak_regime: Fully oxidative ageing in American oak butts; solera system

taste.summary: Dry Sanlúcar Oloroso from Argüeso with full body; rich dried-fruit and rancio complexity from oxidative solera ageing in the bodega's Barrio Bajo cellars.

pairings.why: Oxidative richness of the Oloroso complements the meaty depth of braised lamb.
Crystalline, intensely flavoured old cheese matches the wine's nutty concentration.
Earthy pulse dish benefits from the wine's savoury rancio register.

history.summary: The Las Medallas Oloroso is the complement to the Amontillado in Argüeso's heritage range. Produced under full oxidative ageing from the outset, without biological flor coverage, it develops the characteristic dark colour and rancio complexity of dry Sanlúcar Oloroso.

milestones.event: Las Medallas Oloroso established alongside the Amontillado in the Argüeso award range

wines.description: Argüeso Las Medallas Oloroso: dry, full-bodied Sanlúcar Oloroso with dried fig, rancio and coffee character from oxidative solera ageing.

wines.name: Argüeso Pedro Ximénez
wines.oak_regime: Oxidative ageing in American oak butts from sun-dried PX must; solera system

taste.summary: Classic PX with near-black colour and extreme dried-fruit sweetness; produced from sun-dried Pedro Ximénez grapes in the Sanlúcar bodega.

pairings.why: Classic Spanish dessert pairing; both have caramel-chocolate depth.
Sweet caramel custard echoes the wine's syrupy concentration.
Intense Spanish blue finds sweet-salty tension with the PX.

history.summary: Argüeso PX is produced from Pedro Ximénez grapes dried on straw mats before pressing, concentrating sugars dramatically. The resulting must is fortified to preserve residual sweetness, then aged in American oak solera butts in the Sanlúcar bodega. The PX classification falls under the DO Jerez-Xérès-Sherry rather than the Manzanilla DO.

milestones.event: PX solera established at Bodegas Argüeso

wines.description: Argüeso PX: intensely sweet Pedro Ximénez from Sanlúcar with molasses, raisins and chocolate depth.

wines.name: San León Manzanilla (Herederos de Argüeso)
wines.oak_regime: Biological ageing under flor in American oak butts; Barrio Bajo solera

taste.summary: San León under the Herederos de Argüeso label: the heritage expression of the estate's founding Manzanilla solera from the Barrio Bajo of Sanlúcar.

pairings.dish: Coquinas (small clams) with garlic and parsley
pairings.why: Briny clams and sautéed garlic are the traditional Sanlúcar pairing for San León.
Chilled seafood salad is elevated by the wine's bright acidity and saline register.

history.summary: Herederos de Argüeso (Heirs of Argüeso) is the continuation label of the founding family after León de Argüeso's descendants maintained the original soleras. The San León under this label represents the oldest continuous thread of the estate's 1822 foundation Manzanilla solera.

milestones.event: León de Argüeso establishes original solera in Sanlúcar's Barrio Bajo
Herederos de Argüeso registered as continuation of the founding family estate

wines.description: San León under the Herederos de Argüeso heritage label: crisp, saline Manzanilla from the founding 1822 solera in the Barrio Bajo of Sanlúcar.

wines.name: Reserva de Familia Manzanilla Pasada
wines.oak_regime: Extended biological ageing; flor partially recedes in older butts; solera system

taste.summary: Premium Manzanilla Pasada from the Herederos de Argüeso family archive solera; the extended under-flor ageing develops deeper complexity while retaining the Atlantic salinity characteristic of Sanlúcar.

pairings.why: Natural sweetness of crustaceans highlighted by the wine's saline, nutty depth.

pairings.dish: Salt cod with olive oil (bacalao al pil-pil)
pairings.why: Gelatinous emulsified salt cod is a Spanish classic with aged Manzanilla.
The quintessential Andalusian pairing for fine aged Sanlúcar wine.
