history.summary: The Reserva de Familia Manzanilla Pasada under the Herederos de Argüeso label is the private reserve expression, drawing from the oldest butts in the family's Barrio Bajo solera. The wine is held back until the flor partially recedes, producing the Pasada character that was historically reserved for family use and special occasions.

milestones.event: Foundation of Argüeso family soleras; oldest reserve butts maintained
Reserva de Familia released commercially as a limited label

wines.description: Herederos de Argüeso Reserva de Familia: premium Manzanilla Pasada from the oldest solera butts; walnut, salt and floral complexity from extended under-flor Sanlúcar ageing.

wines.name: Aurora Manzanilla
wines.oak_regime: Biological ageing under flor in American oak butts; solera system in Barrio Alto

pairings.why: Crispy Cádiz shrimp fritters are the canonical local pairing for Aurora Manzanilla.
Chargrilled sardines on the beach are the traditional Andalusian Manzanilla partner.
Acid-marinated anchovies resonate with the wine's bright acidity and sea character.

history.summary: Pedro Romero has produced Aurora Manzanilla since the family bodega's founding in 1860. Situated in the Barrio Alto of Sanlúcar de Barrameda, the cellars benefit from proximity to the Atlantic which sustains active flor year-round. Aurora has a reputation as an unfiltered-focused producer, contributing wines to the en rama movement.

milestones.event: Pedro Romero bodega founded in the Barrio Alto of Sanlúcar
Aurora En Rama bottling introduced alongside the standard Aurora

wines.description: Pedro Romero Aurora Manzanilla: traditional Sanlúcar Barrio Alto Manzanilla, light and saline with chamomile and green almond character.

wines.name: Aurora Manzanilla En Rama
wines.oak_regime: Biological ageing under flor; bottled unfiltered direct from solera

pairings.why: The raw, saline intensity of oysters is matched by the live flor character of en rama.
Oceanic mineral intensity of sea urchin meets the wine's equally intense saline depth.
Light sear on tuna with citrus resonates with en rama's fresh, racy character.

history.summary: Pedro Romero began releasing Aurora En Rama in the early 2000s, tapping the Aurora solera directly in spring when flor coverage is thickest. The unfiltered bottling preserves the live flor yeast and suspended particles that give en rama Manzanilla its distinctive turbid appearance and richer texture compared to filtered releases.

milestones.event: Aurora En Rama first released commercially by Pedro Romero

wines.description: Pedro Romero Aurora En Rama: unfiltered direct-from-solera Manzanilla with live yeast texture; richer and more complex than the standard Aurora.

wines.name: Yuste Manzanilla
wines.oak_regime: Biological ageing under flor in American oak butts; solera system

pairings.why: Manzanilla and lemon-lime soda is the classic Feria de Abril drink in Sanlúcar.
Sanlúcar's pescaíto frito tradition demands a cold glass of Manzanilla.
Briny tapas combination resonates with the wine's saline register.

history.summary: Bodegas Yuste has operated in Sanlúcar de Barrameda since 1805, maintaining traditional biological soleras in the river-facing Atlantic microclimate. The bodega represents the smaller, artisan tier of the Sanlúcar Manzanilla trade, producing for local consumption and the regional hospitality trade.

milestones.event: Bodegas Yuste founded on Avenida Guadalquivir in Sanlúcar

wines.description: Yuste Manzanilla: traditional, affordable Sanlúcar Manzanilla from one of the river-road bodegas; saline, fresh and classic.

wines.name: Yuste Cream
wines.oak_regime: Oloroso base sweetened with PX; solera system

pairings.why: Almond shortbread and caramel cream notes align in both.
Sweet, creamy dessert and the wine's caramel depth are complementary.

history.summary: Yuste Cream is the sweet fortified offering from this small Sanlúcar bodega, made in the traditional style of blending a dry Oloroso base with concentrated Pedro Ximénez to achieve a medium-sweet Cream sherry for the domestic and export market.

milestones.event: Yuste Cream first produced for domestic sale

wines.description: Yuste Cream: traditional medium-sweet sherry from Sanlúcar with caramel, dried apricot and vanilla character.

wines.name: Moscatel Especial
wines.oak_regime: Oxidative ageing in American oak butts; solera system from sandy Chipiona soils

pairings.why: The wine's floral honey sweetness mirrors the accompaniments naturally.
Orange blossom notes align with the orange-zested egg custard of Semana Santa torrijas.
Toasted almond pastry highlights the floral and honeyed register of Moscatel.

history.summary: Chipiona, the coastal town north of Sanlúcar de Barrameda, has a centuries-old tradition of growing Moscatel de Alejandría on its sandy arenales soils. César Florido has been the principal custodian of this tradition since 1887, producing Moscatel wines that differ fundamentally from the raisin-dried PX style. The sandy coastal soils give a distinctive floral, orange-blossom aromatic character not found in PX.

milestones.event: Bodegas César Florido founded in Chipiona; Moscatel production from coastal sandy soils
Moscatel Especial label established for the premium tier

wines.description: César Florido Moscatel Especial: intensely aromatic Chipiona Moscatel with orange blossom, honey and floral character from sandy coastal vineyards.

wines.name: Moscatel Pasas
wines.oak_regime: From sun-dried raisin (pasas) grapes; oxidative ageing in American oak

pairings.why: The raisin and candied fruit complexity of both wine and cake match perfectly.
Rich liver fat with the wine's honey-and-raisin sweetness is a classic French-influenced pairing.
The wine's almond-marzipan note and exotic spice resonate with dark chocolate confection.

history.summary: Moscatel Pasas uses the sun-drying technique typically associated with Pedro Ximénez but applied to Moscatel de Alejandría grapes. The result is a more aromatic, exotic concentrated wine than the PX raisin style; the Moscatel variety's naturally high floral esters persist even through the drying and fortification process. César Florido is one of the few producers regularly bottling this style.

milestones.event: Pasas (raisin) style Moscatel established at César Florido

wines.description: César Florido Moscatel Pasas: raisin-dried Moscatel of extreme aromatic concentration; exotic spice, honey and marmalade notes from Chipiona's sandy coastal soils.

wines.name: Cruz del Mar Cream
wines.oak_regime: Oloroso base with Moscatel sweetening; solera system

pairings.why: Sweet, aromatic wine matches fried dough and rich chocolate.
Floral Moscatel sweetness echoes the honey drizzle over mild cheese.

history.summary: Cruz del Mar (Cross of the Sea) is named for the landmark cross overlooking the Chipiona coast. The Cream style at César Florido uses Moscatel de Alejandría rather than PX for the sweet component, giving it the distinctly floral, orange blossom-tinged aromatic profile that distinguishes Chipiona Moscatel wines from those of Jerez.

milestones.event: Cruz del Mar Cream label first released

wines.description: César Florido Cruz del Mar Cream: Moscatel-sweetened Cream sherry with floral orange blossom aromatics distinguishing it from PX-sweetened styles.

wines.name: Coquinero Fino-Amontillado
wines.oak_regime: Progressive biological-to-oxidative ageing; intermediate between Fino and full Amontillado

taste.summary: Fino-Amontillado intermediate style from El Puerto de Santa María: the flor partially receded, yielding additional almond and light oxidative depth while retaining freshness.

pairings.dish: Grilled prawns and gambas al ajillo
pairings.why: Seafood with garlic and olive oil is the El Puerto classic pairing.
Earthy mushroom bite suits the wine's partly oxidative nutty character.
Salty cured ham and dry sherry is the Andalusian pairing archetype.

history.summary: Coquinero is Osborne's semi-aged sherry in the intermediate Fino-Amontillado category. Founded in 1772 in El Puerto de Santa María by Thomas Osborne Mann, the bodega benefits from the Atlantic influence of the port town, where sea breezes moderate cellar temperatures. Coquinero (meaning a small cooking pot or a Cádiz cook) is a traditional Osborne label.

milestones.event: Osborne founded by Thomas Osborne Mann in El Puerto de Santa María
Coquinero registered as a commercial Fino-Amontillado label

wines.description: Osborne Coquinero: intermediate Fino-Amontillado from El Puerto de Santa María with almond, hazelnut and light oxidative character.

verified.address_quoted: Calle Los Moros 7, El Puerto de Santa María

wines.name: Bailén Oloroso Seco
wines.oak_regime: Oxidative ageing in American oak butts; solera system

taste.summary: Osborne's classic dry Oloroso; full body from long oxidative solera ageing, dark dried-fruit and rancio complexity characteristic of El Puerto's Atlantic-moderated Oloroso style.

pairings.why: Hearty chickpea and meat stew has the weight to stand up to full Oloroso.
Rich, fatty ribs need the wine's oxidative depth and dry finish to balance.
Sharp Balearic island cheese pairs classically with dry Oloroso.

history.summary: Bailén Oloroso takes its name from the Battle of Bailén (1808), a celebrated Spanish victory that made the name synonymous with Andalusian pride. Osborne named this Oloroso after the battle during the Napoleonic era. The wine has been produced as a flagship Oloroso from the El Puerto bodega for over two centuries.

milestones.event: Bailén brand name adopted by Osborne in honour of the Battle of Bailén
Osborne bodega founded in El Puerto de Santa María

wines.description: Osborne Bailén: dry, full-bodied Oloroso named for the 1808 battle; dried fig, walnut and rancio complexity from long El Puerto oxidative ageing.

verified.address_quoted: Calle Los Moros 7, El Puerto de Santa María

wines.name: Amontillado AOS VORS
wines.oak_regime: Extended biological then full oxidative ageing; VORS solera (average over 30 years)

taste.finish: very long, complex and dry with warming alcohol
taste.summary: VORS Amontillado from Osborne's deepest soleras: 30-plus year average age gives extraordinary rancio concentration and complexity in the dry El Puerto Atlantic microclimate.

pairings.why: VORS complexity warrants full attention without food distraction.
Intense farmhouse cheese flavours match the wine's concentrated depth.

history.summary: The AOS (named for the Amontillado Osborne Solera designation) represents the apex of Osborne's Amontillado range. A VORS designation requires the wine to have an average age of over 30 years across the full solera scale, certified by the Consejo Regulador. Released in very limited quantities from the El Puerto de Santa María bodega.

milestones.event: Foundation Amontillado solera established at Osborne
AOS VORS released commercially under modern packaging

wines.description: Osborne AOS VORS Amontillado: 30-plus year average age; extraordinary rancio and hazelnut complexity from Osborne's oldest El Puerto solera.

verified.address_quoted: Calle Los Moros 7, El Puerto de Santa María

wines.name: Pedro Ximénez 1827
wines.oak_regime: Oxidative ageing in American oak butts; solera from foundation year 1827

taste.summary: PX from Osborne's 1827 solera: intensely concentrated and syrupy with espresso, dark chocolate and prune character from nearly two centuries of solera refinement.

pairings.why: The standard PX service; contrast of cold vanilla and warm molasses-intensity.
Coffee and dark chocolate in both wine and cake are amplified by the pairing.
Sweet-salty contrast: the wine's intense sweetness cuts through mild blue cheese.

history.summary: Osborne Pedro Ximénez 1827 references the year the foundation solera was laid down, drawing from Osborne's earliest verified solera records. The 1827 date makes this one of the historically referenced PX soleras in the Jerez DO. Produced from sun-dried PX grapes and aged in the El Puerto de Santa María bodega.

milestones.event: Osborne's Pedro Ximénez foundation solera established
PX 1827 label repackaged and relaunched in collector format

wines.description: Osborne Pedro Ximénez 1827: solera founded 1827; intensely sweet with espresso, molasses and dark chocolate from nearly two centuries of El Puerto oxidative ageing.

verified.address_quoted: Calle Los Moros 7, El Puerto de Santa María

wines.name: Fino El Puerto
wines.oak_regime: Biological ageing under flor in American oak butts; riverside solera system

taste.summary: Fino from the riverside bodega in El Puerto de Santa María; lighter and more delicate than Jerez-city Finos due to the Atlantic coastal influence on the cellar microclimate.

pairings.why: Grilled prawns with sea salt and lemon are the El Puerto waterfront classic.
Crispy mixed seafood with coarse salt is the traditional Cádiz provincial pairing.
Briny, fatty snacks are elevated by the wine's clean cutting acidity.

history.summary: Gutiérrez Colosía's Fino is produced from their riverside bodega on the Avenida Bajamar in El Puerto de Santa María, where the Guadalete river meets the sea. The Atlantic conditions of this location moderate cellar temperatures and sustain flor yeast activity, producing a Fino that is lighter and more delicate than typical Jerez-city expressions.

milestones.event: Gutiérrez Colosía bodega established on the waterfront at El Puerto de Santa María

wines.description: Gutiérrez Colosía Fino El Puerto: light, delicate riverside bodega Fino with Atlantic freshness and saline character from the El Puerto waterfront cellars.

wines.name: Amontillado
wines.oak_regime: Progressive biological-to-oxidative ageing in American oak solera

taste.summary: El Puerto Amontillado with hazelnut and light caramel character; the Atlantic microclimate of the riverside bodega gives a lighter, fresher character than inland Jerez Amontillado.

pairings.why: Garlic prawns with a glass of dry Amontillado is a traditional El Puerto combination.
Classic dry-sherry aperitif accompaniments.
Creamy mushroom croquettes and nutty Amontillado are a natural bridge.

history.summary: Gutiérrez Colosía's Amontillado follows the standard El Puerto progression from Fino biological ageing to oxidative. The riverside location moderates the cellar's temperature and humidity, producing an Amontillado that retains a lighter, more delicate character compared to the richer, more concentrated Amontillados of Jerez's inland bodegas.

milestones.event: Amontillado solera established at the riverside El Puerto bodega

wines.description: Gutiérrez Colosía Amontillado: dry El Puerto Amontillado with hazelnut, toffee and Atlantic-fresh saline character from the riverside bodega.

wines.name: Oloroso Sangre y Trabajadero
wines.oak_regime: Fully oxidative ageing in American oak butts; Sangre y Trabajadero pago; solera system

taste.summary: Oloroso named after the Sangre y Trabajadero pago (Blood and Toil): full-bodied, dry and concentrated with the characteristic rancio depth of old El Puerto oxidative soleras.

pairings.why: Rabo de toro is traditionally cooked and served with the same Oloroso style.
Gamey braised meats require the depth of a full Oloroso.

pairings.dish: Aged Payoyo goat cheese from Cádiz
pairings.why: Local Cádiz cheese with earthy intensity pairs with the dry oxidative depth.

history.summary: Sangre y Trabajadero is one of the named vineyards (pagos) in the Jerez wine-growing area, referenced in the name of this Gutiérrez Colosía Oloroso. The evocative name (Blood and Toil) captures the traditional stoicism of the Jerez viticulture in the hot Andalusian summer. The wine is produced exclusively from Palomino Fino grapes grown on the albariza soils of the pago.

milestones.event: Oloroso solera from Sangre y Trabajadero pago established at Gutiérrez Colosía

wines.description: Gutiérrez Colosía Sangre y Trabajadero Oloroso: single-pago dry Oloroso with full body; dried fig, rancio and leather from old El Puerto riverside soleras.

wines.name: Pedro Ximénez
wines.oak_regime: Oxidative ageing in American oak butts from sun-dried must; solera system

taste.summary: Classic Pedro Ximénez from the El Puerto riverside bodega: near-black, intensely sweet and concentrated with molasses, dark chocolate and dried-fruit character.

pairings.why: The traditional PX service contrasts creamy cold with syrupy intensity.
Dark chocolate richness is mirrored and amplified by the wine's cocoa register.

history.summary: Gutiérrez Colosía's Pedro Ximénez is produced from grapes dried on straw mats in the Montilla area, then vinified and aged in the El Puerto bodega. The wine represents the sweet complement to the bodega's dry range and has been part of the El Puerto portfolio since the bodega's foundation.

milestones.event: PX solera established at Gutiérrez Colosía's El Puerto bodega

wines.description: Gutiérrez Colosía Pedro Ximénez: near-black, intensely sweet PX from El Puerto with molasses, espresso and dried-fruit character.

wines.name: Palo Cortado
wines.oak_regime: Spontaneous flor die-back followed by oxidative ageing; American oak butts

taste.finish: long with the characteristic Palo Cortado tension
taste.summary: Palo Cortado from El Puerto's riverside bodega; the Amontillado aromatics with Oloroso palate weight, lighter and more Atlantic in character than inland Jerez equivalents.

pairings.why: The wine's full body with aromatic finesse bridges the richness of roasted pork.
Smoky Basque cheese meets the wine's oxidative depth and hazelnut aromatics.

history.summary: Gutiérrez Colosía's Palo Cortado arises from casks in the riverside bodega where the flor yeast spontaneously dies, diverting the wine's development from biological to oxidative ageing. The El Puerto location means these Palo Cortados have a characteristic lighter Atlantic character compared to those from the warmer interior of Jerez.

milestones.event: Riverside El Puerto bodega established; Palo Cortado casks first identified

wines.description: Gutiérrez Colosía Palo Cortado: Atlantic-influenced El Puerto expression of the rare intermediate style; Amontillado nose with full Oloroso body.

wines.name: Pavón Fino
wines.oak_regime: Biological ageing under flor in American oak butts; El Puerto de Santa María solera

pairings.why: El Puerto's waterfront shellfish tradition pairs naturally with Fino.
Local Cádiz coastal fry-up suits a bright, dry Fino perfectly.
Almond soup with the freshness of cold Fino is a traditional Andalusian combination.

history.summary: Caballero has been based in El Puerto de Santa María since 1830. The Pavón Fino takes its name from the peacock (pavo real / pavón) in a traditional heraldic reference. El Puerto's Atlantic microclimate makes its Fino production somewhat lighter than the inland Jerez style, a distinction that has characterised Caballero's wines since the 19th century.

milestones.event: Bodegas Caballero founded in El Puerto de Santa María
Pavón Fino label registered commercially

wines.description: Caballero Pavón Fino: Atlantic-influenced El Puerto Fino with almond, sea salt and lemon freshness.

verified.address_quoted: El Puerto de Santa María, Cádiz, Spain

wines.name: Burdon Oloroso
wines.oak_regime: Oxidative ageing in American oak butts; El Puerto solera system

pairings.why: Herbed lamb needs the wine's oxidative depth and long dry finish.
Creamy sheep's cheese spread with intense flavour is a classic Oloroso pairing.

history.summary: The Burdon label is named after John Burdon, an English merchant who partnered with Caballero in the 19th century to develop the El Puerto export trade. The Oloroso reflects the house's long history of producing fortified wines for northern European markets where the richer, drier styles were particularly prized.

milestones.event: Burdon brand established with English merchant John Burdon

wines.description: Caballero Burdon Oloroso: dry El Puerto Oloroso with dried fig, walnut and rancio character; named for the historic English merchant partnership.

verified.address_quoted: El Puerto de Santa María, Cádiz, Spain

wines.name: Macharnudo Pago Amontillado
wines.oak_regime: Biological then oxidative ageing; sourced from grapes grown in the Macharnudo pago

pairings.why: The pago character and mineral depth of this Amontillado match pork's fat and flavour.
Artichoke's tricky wine-pairing challenge is solved by dry Amontillado's mineral character.

history.summary: Macharnudo is the most famous individual vineyard pago in the Jerez Triangle, producing Palomino Fino grapes from deep albariza chalk soils with exceptional mineral character. Caballero's Macharnudo Pago Amontillado draws from grapes grown in this single high-reputation vineyard, producing a more structured Amontillado than typical solera-blended expressions.

milestones.event: Caballero begins sourcing from Macharnudo pago for premium wines
Macharnudo Pago Amontillado released as a named single-vineyard label

wines.description: Caballero Macharnudo Pago Amontillado: single-vineyard from the celebrated Macharnudo chalk pago; mineral-edged, dry and structured.

verified.address_quoted: El Puerto de Santa María, Cádiz, Spain

wines.name: Fino El Puerto
wines.oak_regime: Biological ageing under flor in American oak butts; El Puerto de Santa María solera

pairings.why: Ocean-fresh shellfish and Atlantic Fino are a natural El Puerto combination.
The simplest and most traditional Andalusian Fino companion.

history.summary: Bodegas Obregón has operated in El Puerto de Santa María since 1890, maintaining traditional biological Fino and Manzanilla soleras in the Atlantic-influenced port town environment. A family-run almacenista bodega contributing to the El Puerto sherry trade.

milestones.event: Bodegas Obregón founded in El Puerto de Santa María

wines.description: Obregón Fino El Puerto: Atlantic-influenced El Puerto Fino from a small family bodega; light, saline and refreshing.

wines.name: Manzanilla
wines.oak_regime: Biological ageing under flor in American oak butts

pairings.why: Sanlúcar brine and the oyster's iodine-salinity are complementary.
Light crispy clams suit the wine's refreshing salinity.

history.summary: Obregón produces both a Fino (under the Jerez DO) and a Manzanilla (under the Sanlúcar DO), reflecting the dual Atlantic-town tradition of El Puerto bodegas that historically also produced Sanlúcar-classified wines. The Manzanilla is the lighter, more saline expression of the biological ageing tradition.

milestones.event: Manzanilla solera established at Bodegas Obregón

wines.description: Obregón Manzanilla: traditional Sanlúcar-classified wine from this El Puerto bodega; saline, chamomile and sea-spray freshness.

wines.name: La Cigarrera Manzanilla
wines.oak_regime: Biological ageing under flor in American oak butts; Barrio Alto solera system

pairings.dish: Gambas crudas (raw prawns) with sea salt
pairings.why: The simplest El Puerto seafood pairing for cold, dry Manzanilla.
Local Sanlúcar Barrameda cheese with the house Manzanilla is the traditional pairing.
Sanlúcar street-tapa with the house Manzanilla is the traditional local combination.

history.summary: La Cigarrera (The Cigar Maker) takes its name from the tradition of cigar-rolling women who worked in the Sanlúcar tobacco trade in the 18th and 19th centuries. The bodega was founded in 1758 in the Barrio Bajo district of Sanlúcar, making it one of the oldest continuously operating Manzanilla houses. The Plaza de las Madejas quarter where the bodega sits was historically associated with the tobacco and wine trades.

milestones.event: Bodegas La Cigarrera founded in the Barrio Bajo of Sanlúcar de Barrameda

wines.description: La Cigarrera Manzanilla: traditional Sanlúcar Manzanilla from one of the oldest surviving bodegas (1758); saline, chamomile and sea-spray character.

wines.name: La Cigarrera Manzanilla Pasada En Rama
wines.oak_regime: Extended biological ageing with partial flor recession; bottled unfiltered

pairings.why: Intensely saline cured fish meets the wine's amplified brine and almond depth.
Clean shellfish with herbed oil suits the wine's saline-herb register.
Mojama is the traditional Sanlúcar en rama partner; salt-dried tuna and aged Manzanilla.

history.summary: La Cigarrera's en rama range has become one of the most sought-after expressions of unfiltered Manzanilla from Sanlúcar's small bodegas. The Pasada En Rama is drawn from older butts in the solera where flor activity has naturally decreased, producing the intermediate Manzanilla Pasada character before bottling without filtration to preserve the live complexity.

milestones.event: La Cigarrera solera established in the Barrio Bajo of Sanlúcar
Manzanilla Pasada En Rama released as a limited unfiltered expression

wines.description: La Cigarrera Manzanilla Pasada En Rama: unfiltered extended-ageing Manzanilla from Sanlúcar's oldest surviving bodegas; complex, saline and textural.

wines.name: La Cigarrera Amontillado En Rama
wines.oak_regime: Full biological-to-oxidative transition; bottled unfiltered; American oak solera

pairings.why: The wine's saline depth and almond character are a natural match for the coastal salt-dried fish tradition.
Crispy potato with garlic emulsion suits the wine's savoury, mineral weight.

history.summary: La Cigarrera's en rama philosophy extends to their Amontillado, drawn from the full biological-to-oxidative solera and bottled without filtration. The practice preserves the live volatile compounds that the filtration process typically removes, giving the wine a more complex, slightly turbid character valued by enthusiasts of unfiltered sherry.

milestones.event: Amontillado solera transition established at La Cigarrera
Amontillado En Rama released as a limited unfiltered bottling

wines.description: La Cigarrera Amontillado En Rama: unfiltered Sanlúcar Amontillado from a 1758 solera; saline, nutty and complex with live yeast texture preserved by en rama bottling.

wines.name: La Cigarrera Oloroso En Rama
wines.oak_regime: Oxidative ageing in American oak solera; bottled unfiltered

pairings.why: Fatty, rich pork requires the dry oxidative weight of a full Oloroso.

pairings.dish: Cured Payoyo cheese from the Sierra de Grazalema
pairings.why: Local Cádiz provincial cheese with the same mineral-saline terroir reference.

history.summary: La Cigarrera's Oloroso En Rama represents the bodega's fully oxidative tier bottled without filtration. The combination of old Sanlúcar solera and en rama bottling produces a wine that captures both the marine Atlantic character of the location and the deep oxidative complexity of long solera ageing.

milestones.event: Oloroso solera established at La Cigarrera, Sanlúcar
Oloroso En Rama released as a limited unfiltered expression

wines.description: La Cigarrera Oloroso En Rama: unfiltered dry Oloroso from Sanlúcar's 1758 solera; full oxidative complexity with dried fig, walnut and saline depth.

wines.name: Gabriela Manzanilla
wines.oak_regime: Biological ageing under flor in American oak butts; Sanlúcar Barrio Bajo solera

pairings.why: Fried fresh anchovies are the traditional Cádiz Manzanilla pairing.
Classic tapas accompaniment for any fresh Sanlúcar Manzanilla.
Sanlúcar's famous prawns are the local pairing partner for any Barrio Bajo Manzanilla.

history.summary: Bodegas Sánchez Ayala was founded in 1852 at Calle Misericordia in Sanlúcar de Barrameda. Gabriela is the name given to the bodega's core Manzanilla, a traditional biological solera wine from the Barrio Bajo quarter. The bodega is independent of Bodegas Hidalgo La Gitana and the La Gitana brand, despite the superficial similarity suggested by the combined entry in some databases.

milestones.event: Bodegas Sánchez Ayala founded at Calle Misericordia, Sanlúcar
Gabriela Manzanilla label established as the house flagship

wines.description: Sánchez Ayala Gabriela Manzanilla: traditional Sanlúcar Barrio Bajo Manzanilla with sea salt, chamomile and green apple freshness.

wines.name: Palo Cortado Orgánico
wines.oak_regime: Spontaneous biological-to-oxidative transition; organic solera in American oak

taste.summary: Organic Palo Cortado: the rarest sherry style from a certified organic producer; hazelnut aromatics over a full, oxidative body with the characteristic tension of the intermediate classification.

pairings.why: Organic-farmed lamb with herbed roasting echoes the wine's earthy mineral and dried-herb character.

pairings.dish: Hard aged cheese with quince paste
pairings.why: Palo Cortado's Amontillado-Oloroso duality bridges the cheese's saltiness and the sweet quince.

history.summary: Bodegas Robles is a certified organic producer in the Montilla region near Córdoba who also holds DO Jerez-Xérès-Sherry certification for their Palo Cortado and PX wines. The organic certification distinguishes them as one of the few sherry-category producers with formal organic accreditation. Their Palo Cortado arises from natural flor die-back in the organic solera.

milestones.event: Bodegas Robles founded in Montilla with organic farming principles
DO Jerez-Xérès-Sherry certification obtained; organic Palo Cortado released

wines.description: Bodegas Robles Palo Cortado Orgánico: rare organic-certified Palo Cortado with hazelnut aromatics and full oxidative body; among the few certified organic sherries.

wines.name: Pedro Ximénez Orgánico
wines.oak_regime: Oxidative ageing in American oak from sun-dried organic PX must; solera system

taste.summary: Organic Pedro Ximénez: the same intensely sweet, near-black style as conventional PX but produced without synthetic pesticides or herbicides on the Montilla PX vineyards.

pairings.why: Organic-farmed dark chocolate with the wine's coffee and cocoa register.
The standard PX pairing; warm syrupy intensity over cold cream.
Intense sweet-salty tension from organically farmed components.

history.summary: Bodegas Robles PX is produced from Pedro Ximénez grapes grown organically in the Montilla region, where the variety is native and produces the grapes that dry most readily on esparto mats. The organic certification from a sherry-category producer is unusual and positions Robles PX as a premium alternative for consumers seeking environmentally certified fortified wines.

milestones.event: Robles PX solera established
Organic certification obtained for PX wines

wines.description: Bodegas Robles Pedro Ximénez Orgánico: certified organic PX from Montilla with the full intensity of molasses, espresso and dried fruit.

wines.name: Vergara Manzanilla
wines.oak_regime: Biological ageing under flor in American oak butts; Sanlúcar solera system

pairings.why: Grilled prawns and Sanlúcar Manzanilla is the regional standard pairing.
Pescaíto frito with a glass of cold Manzanilla is the Cádiz coastal tradition.

history.summary: Bodegas Vergara has maintained Manzanilla soleras in Sanlúcar de Barrameda since 1891. A small producer contributing to the Consejo Regulador directory, the bodega is distinct from Palomino and Vergara (Palomino & Vergara), which is a Jerez-based house. Bodegas Vergara is a Sanlúcar-classified Manzanilla producer.

milestones.event: Bodegas Vergara founded in Sanlúcar de Barrameda

wines.description: Vergara Manzanilla: traditional Sanlúcar Manzanilla from a small artisan producer; light, saline and fresh with chamomile and sea-spray character.

wines.name: Reyes Gutiérrez Manzanilla
wines.oak_regime: Biological ageing under flor in American oak butts; Barrio Alto solera

pairings.why: Cádiz shrimp fritters are the traditional street-food Manzanilla pairing.
Light salty snacks suit the wine's refreshing, dry mineral character.

history.summary: Bodegas Reyes Gutiérrez was founded in 1920 in the Barrio Alto of Sanlúcar de Barrameda, near the mouth of the Guadalquivir river where Atlantic breezes sustain continuous flor activity. A small family bodega producing traditional Manzanilla for the local hospitality and domestic trade.

milestones.event: Bodegas Reyes Gutiérrez founded in Barrio Alto, Sanlúcar de Barrameda

wines.description: Reyes Gutiérrez Manzanilla: small-producer Sanlúcar Manzanilla from the Barrio Alto; sea-salt, chamomile and refreshing Atlantic character.

wines.name: García de Velasco Fino
wines.oak_regime: Biological ageing under flor in American oak butts; Jerez de la Frontera almacenista solera

pairings.why: Classic dry sherry and charcuterie pairing.
Pescaíto frito is the traditional Andalusian Fino accompaniment.

history.summary: Bodegas García de Velasco has operated as a small almacenista (stockholder bodega) in Jerez de la Frontera since 1886, contributing Fino and Amontillado barrels to larger houses. The almacenista tradition is a distinctive feature of the Jerez sherry system, where small bodegas maintain high-quality soleras sold to négociants.

milestones.event: Bodegas García de Velasco founded as an almacenista in Jerez

wines.description: García de Velasco Fino: small almacenista Jerez Fino with fresh almond, mineral and bread-dough character.

verified.address_quoted: Jerez de la Frontera, Cádiz, Spain

wines.name: García de Velasco Amontillado
wines.oak_regime: Biological then oxidative ageing in American oak solera; almacenista production

pairings.why: A classic Spanish tapa combination that showcases dry Amontillado's savouriness.
Salty and nutty, these partners amplify the wine's core flavour register.

history.summary: García de Velasco's Amontillado is the oxidative tier of this small almacenista bodega, aged through the full biological-to-oxidative progression in their Jerez city-centre soleras. Historically the wine was sold in barrel to larger Jerez houses; the bottled release under the García de Velasco label is a more recent development.

milestones.event: Amontillado solera established at García de Velasco almacenista bodega

wines.description: García de Velasco Amontillado: small almacenista Jerez Amontillado with hazelnut, toffee and citrus-mineral character from old city-centre soleras.

verified.address_quoted: Jerez de la Frontera, Cádiz, Spain

wines.name: Bay-Alba Amontillado
wines.oak_regime: Biological then oxidative ageing in American oak solera; small almacenista-style production

pairings.dish: Sopa de cebolla gratinada (onion soup gratin)
pairings.why: Dry Amontillado and French-style onion soup is a classic combination.

pairings.dish: Almonds with dried figs and cheese
pairings.why: Classic Andalusian tapa plate for a glass of dry Amontillado.

history.summary: Bodegas Bay-Alba is a small Jerez almacenista bodega founded in 1950, contributing aged Amontillado and Pedro Ximénez to the solera ecosystem of the Jerez Triangle. The bodega supplies barrel wine to larger houses and bottles a small proportion under their own label.

milestones.event: Bodegas Bay-Alba established as an almacenista producer in Jerez de la Frontera

wines.description: Bay-Alba Amontillado: small Jerez almacenista Amontillado with walnut, toffee and dry mineral character.

verified.address_quoted: Jerez de la Frontera, Cádiz, Spain

wines.name: Bay-Alba Pedro Ximénez
wines.oak_regime: Oxidative ageing in American oak butts from sun-dried PX must

pairings.why: PX poured over or alongside vanilla ice cream is the classic service.
Caramel-focused dessert and PX sweetness are natural partners.

history.summary: Bay-Alba PX is the sweet complement to their Amontillado, produced from Pedro Ximénez grapes dried in the Montilla area before transport to Jerez for fortification and ageing. Small almacenista bodegas like Bay-Alba contribute to the diversity of the Jerez sweet wine ecosystem.

milestones.event: PX solera established at Bodegas Bay-Alba

wines.description: Bay-Alba Pedro Ximénez: small Jerez almacenista PX with molasses, espresso and dried fruit sweetness.

verified.address_quoted: Jerez de la Frontera, Cádiz, Spain

wines.name: González Obregón Amontillado
wines.oak_regime: Biological then oxidative ageing in American oak solera; almacenista production for Lustau and others

pairings.why: Dry Amontillado and charcuterie is the foundational Andalusian aperitif pairing.
Dry Amontillado handles artichoke's famously difficult flavour pairing with mineral precision.

history.summary: González Obregón has operated as an almacenista bodega in Jerez since 1895, supplying aged Amontillado to larger négociant houses including Lustau under the Almacenista programme. The programme, which bottles single-almacenista wines under their own names, brought international recognition to small bodegas like González Obregón.

milestones.event: González Obregón almacenista bodega founded in Jerez
Amontillado included in Lustau's Almacenista bottling programme

wines.description: González Obregón Amontillado: Jerez almacenista Amontillado historically supplied to Lustau's Almacenista programme; dry, nutty and minerally from old city-centre soleras.

verified.address_quoted: Jerez de la Frontera, Cádiz, Spain

wines.name: Miguel Martín Fino
wines.oak_regime: Biological ageing under flor in American oak butts; Jerez almacenista-scale solera

pairings.why: Classic Andalusian aperitif combination.
The simplest and most traditional Fino accompaniment.

history.summary: Miguel Martín is a small Jerez producer founded in 1960, contributing to the almacenista network with Fino and Amontillado from soleras in the historic bodega quarter of Jerez de la Frontera. The bodega supplies bulk and bottled wine to the trade.

milestones.event: Miguel Martín bodega founded in Jerez de la Frontera

wines.description: Miguel Martín Fino: small Jerez almacenista Fino; light, fresh and clean with almond, citrus and mineral character.

verified.address_quoted: Jerez de la Frontera, Cádiz, Spain

wines.name: Miguel Martín Amontillado
wines.oak_regime: Biological then oxidative ageing in American oak solera

pairings.why: Dry Amontillado and aged cheeses is the classic Andalusian aperitif combination.
Roasted almonds amplify the wine's core hazelnut register.

history.summary: Miguel Martín's Amontillado is the oxidative complement to their Fino, produced from the same Jerez de la Frontera solera system by allowing the biological phase to complete its natural progression to oxidative development.

milestones.event: Amontillado solera established at Miguel Martín

wines.description: Miguel Martín Amontillado: small Jerez almacenista Amontillado; dry, nutty and mineral.

verified.address_quoted: Jerez de la Frontera, Cádiz, Spain

wines.name: Barbadillo Manzanilla Clásica
wines.oak_regime: Biological ageing under flor in American oak butts; classic solera system

pairings.why: Designed as an easy-drinking aperitif companion for a broad range of tapas.
Chilled Atlantic prawns and cold Manzanilla is the simplest Sanlúcar pleasure.

history.summary: Barbadillo Manzanilla Clásica is the house's most accessible everyday Manzanilla, designed for high-volume distribution in the Spanish domestic market and export. It represents the Sanlúcar style at its most approachable price point while maintaining the biological ageing under flor that defines Manzanilla.

milestones.event: Barbadillo Manzanilla solera first established
Clásica range created as a branded everyday tier

wines.description: Barbadillo Manzanilla Clásica: everyday entry-level Manzanilla from the largest Sanlúcar producer; fresh, saline and approachable.

wines.name: Amontillado Viejo
wines.oak_regime: Extended biological then long oxidative ageing; old solera with VOS classification potential

taste.summary: Old Amontillado from the Hidalgo Sanlúcar solera: extended ageing on the scale between standard Amontillado and VORS, the saline coastal terroir persisting through deep oxidative complexity.

pairings.why: The wine's umami oxidative depth and sea-salt edge elevate earthy wild fungi.

pairings.dish: Aged Manchego Gran Reserva with quince
pairings.why: Old cheese and old sherry create a flavour exchange of salt, nut and mineral.

history.summary: Hidalgo's Amontillado Viejo (Old Amontillado) is drawn from the deeper tiers of the Sanlúcar solera, representing wine that has completed a full biological phase as Manzanilla before transitioning to extended oxidative development. The wine sits at the upper end of the Amontillado spectrum without reaching the formal VORS designation.

milestones.event: Foundation solera established at Bodegas Hidalgo
Amontillado Viejo released as a named premium tier

wines.description: Hidalgo Amontillado Viejo: old Sanlúcar Amontillado from the deepest tiers of the 1792 solera; hazelnut, sea salt and light rancio complexity.

verified.address_quoted: Calle Banda Playa 42, Sanlúcar de Barrameda

wines.name: Fino Quinta
wines.oak_regime: Biological ageing under flor in American oak butts; El Puerto solera

taste.summary: Osborne's flagship Fino from El Puerto de Santa María: light, delicate and Atlantic-fresh with the characteristic almond and sea-salt profile of the port town's biological ageing tradition.

pairings.why: El Puerto's famous harbour prawns and chilled Fino is the local tradition.
Acid-marinated fresh anchovies and Fino is a classic Andalusian combination.
Light battered squid suits the wine's fresh cutting acidity.

history.summary: Fino Quinta is Osborne's primary Fino expression, produced in El Puerto de Santa María where Atlantic breezes moderate cellar temperatures. The Quinta name references the quinta criadera (fifth row) of the traditional solera system. Osborne has produced Fino from this El Puerto site continuously since the bodega's founding in 1772.

milestones.event: Osborne founded in El Puerto de Santa María; Fino biological solera established
Fino Quinta label registered as the house's primary Fino brand

wines.description: Osborne Fino Quinta: Atlantic El Puerto Fino with almond, chamomile and sea-salt character; the house flagship since 1772.

verified.address_quoted: Calle Los Moros 7, El Puerto de Santa María

wines.name: Solera BC 200 Brandy
wines.oak_regime: Long solera ageing in American and Spanish oak; BC200 solera designation

taste.summary: Brandy de Jerez from Osborne's BC 200 solera; rich, dried-fruit and caramel character from extended American oak ageing in the Jerez solera system.

pairings.why: Rich caramel and chocolate in both the brandy and confection.
Brandy de Jerez and coffee is a classic post-dinner Andalusian tradition.

history.summary: Osborne's Brandy de Jerez is as central to the bodega's identity as its sherries. The BC 200 designation refers to a specific long-aged solera. Brandy de Jerez undergoes solera ageing in the same American oak butts previously used for sherry, absorbing the oxidative complexity of the sherry wood.

milestones.event: Osborne brandy distillation begun alongside sherry production
BC200 solera established for long-aged Brandy de Jerez

wines.description: Osborne Solera BC 200: aged Brandy de Jerez from a 200-year solera; rich caramel, dried fruit and warm oak character from the El Puerto bodega.

verified.address_quoted: Calle Los Moros 7, El Puerto de Santa María

wines.name: Mari Pepa Cream
wines.oak_regime: Oloroso base with PX sweetening; riverside bodega solera

taste.summary: Cream sherry from the Gutiérrez Colosía riverside bodega under the Mari Pepa label; medium-sweet, caramel-toned and approachable with the Atlantic character of El Puerto.

pairings.why: The sweet Cream sherry and candied-fruit cake are an Andalusian holiday pairing.
Fresh fruit tart and the wine's dried-fruit sweetness bridge the fresh-versus-concentrated contrast.

history.summary: Mari Pepa is a traditional Andalusian name used for the Gutiérrez Colosía Cream sherry. The label has been part of the El Puerto riverside bodega's range since the mid-20th century, offering a medium-sweet fortified wine for the domestic and export Cream category.

milestones.event: Cream style solera established at Gutiérrez Colosía
Mari Pepa brand name registered

wines.description: Gutiérrez Colosía Mari Pepa Cream: approachable medium-sweet Cream sherry from the El Puerto riverside bodega with caramel, dried apricot and vanilla character.

wines.name: Soleras Familiares Amontillado
wines.oak_regime: Extended biological-to-oxidative ageing from old family solera butts; premium tier

taste.finish: very long, saline-nutty with Atlantic freshness
taste.summary: Premium Amontillado from Gutiérrez Colosía's oldest family solera butts: the Atlantic El Puerto microclimate gives the wine a lighter, more delicate character than Jerez city-centre equivalents at comparable age.

pairings.why: The wine's Atlantic sea-salt depth and mineral freshness complement premium grilled fish.
Nutty oxidative wine with a nutty aged cheese creates coherence.

history.summary: The Soleras Familiares (Family Soleras) range at Gutiérrez Colosía draws from the oldest butts maintained at the riverside El Puerto bodega since its founding. This premium tier represents the bodega's deepest archive of biological-to-oxidative ageing, distinct from the standard range by greater age and complexity.

milestones.event: Family solera butts established at the El Puerto riverside bodega
Soleras Familiares range created as a premium tier

wines.description: Gutiérrez Colosía Soleras Familiares Amontillado: premium El Puerto Amontillado from old family soleras; Atlantic-fresh, saline-nutty complexity.

wines.name: Delgado Zuleta Oloroso
wines.oak_regime: Oxidative ageing in American oak butts; Sanlúcar solera

taste.finish: long, dry and warming with saline note
taste.summary: Dry Sanlúcar Oloroso from Delgado Zuleta's oldest solera; the Atlantic coastal terroir adds a persistent saline note to the standard dried-fruit and rancio oxidative profile.

pairings.why: Hearty chickpea and meat stew requires the full oxidative weight of a dry Oloroso.
Intense aged Castilian sheep's-milk cheese and dry Oloroso are a classic inland-Andalusian pairing.

history.summary: Delgado Zuleta produces a dry Oloroso alongside their better-known Manzanilla and Amontillado range. The Sanlúcar Oloroso is distinctive for retaining the coastal saline terroir even through full oxidative development, reflecting the unique maritime microclimate of Sanlúcar de Barrameda.

milestones.event: Oloroso solera established at Delgado Zuleta's Sanlúcar bodega

wines.description: Delgado Zuleta Oloroso: dry Sanlúcar Oloroso with the Atlantic saline edge of the Barrameda coast; dried fig, walnut and rancio depth.

verified.address_quoted: Avda. Rocío Jurado, s/n - 11540 Sanlúcar de Barrameda

wines.name: San León Manzanilla En Rama
wines.oak_regime: Biological ageing under flor; bottled unfiltered directly from the San León solera

taste.summary: Unfiltered version of the San León: richer and more complex than the standard filtered bottling with live flor yeast bringing extra depth to the characteristic Barrio Bajo saline character.

pairings.why: The wine's saline depth and flor complexity amplify the sweet mineral ocean flavour of coquinas.
Famous Sanlúcar bay prawns and en rama Manzanilla is the prestige local combination.

history.summary: Argüeso began releasing San León En Rama as part of the broader Sanlúcar movement toward unfiltered seasonal Manzanilla bottlings. The En Rama is drawn from the San León solera in spring when flor activity is at its annual peak, preserving the live yeast and volatile compounds that filtration removes.

milestones.event: San León solera established at Bodegas Argüeso
San León En Rama first released as a seasonal spring bottling

wines.description: Argüeso San León Manzanilla En Rama: seasonal unfiltered spring release with live flor texture; richer and more complex than the standard San León.
